proposals.title: Rhizosphere redox dynamics modulate arsenic speciation, mobility, and phytotoxicity in constructed wetlands: A test of the As(V)-dominance paradigm.
proposals.motivation: Arsenic contamination of water and soils is a major global environmental concern. Constructed wetlands are promising for arsenic remediation, but their design often assumes arsenate [As(V)] is the primary toxic and mobile species, overlooking the complex redox transformations mediated by wetland plant rhizospheres. A pre-2026 knowledge gap exists in quantifying how interspecific plant diversity influences these rhizosphere processes, thereby affecting the relative ecotoxicity of As(III) and As(V).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the toxicity and mobility of arsenic in wetland rhizospheres are not solely determined by As(V), but are dynamically regulated by plant species-specific root exudates and associated microbial activity, which control the reduction of As(V) to As(III) and subsequent phytoavailability.
proposals.method: We will establish a controlled rhizotron experiment with three contrasting wetland plant species (e.g., Juncus, Typha, Phragmites) grown in a contaminated soil. Treatments will involve separate additions of As(III) and As(V). Using planar optodes and diffusive gradients in thin-films (DGT), we will map real-time O2 and pH dynamics and measure labile As species at the root-soil interface. Sequential chemical extraction and HPLC-ICP-MS will quantify arsenic speciation in pore water and bulk soil. Plant uptake and physiological stress markers (e.g., chlorophyll fluorescence, biomass) will be correlated with rhizosphere chemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based understanding of arsenic biogeochemistry in the plant-soil interface of wetlands, directly linking root-mediated redox changes to arsenic speciation and toxicity. It will generate critical data to refine phytoremediation models and practices by moving beyond the simplistic assumption of As(V) as the sole concern.
proposals.novelty: While arsenic speciation is well-studied in bulk soils, the novelty lies in explicitly testing the 'As(V)-dominance' paradigm *within the rhizosphere* of diverse wetland plants, integrating real-time chemical imaging with plant physiological responses to disentangle the roles of As(III) and As(V).

proposals.title: Spatio-temporal dynamics of rhizosphere pH and O2 in cereal-legume agroforestry systems: A planar optode study on arsenic redox mobilization
proposals.motivation: Arsenic (As) contamination in soils poses a significant risk to food security and human health, with its bioavailability strongly controlled by redox and pH conditions in the rhizosphere. Pre-2026 research by Hinsinger's group highlights that plant diversity (e.g., intercropping, agroforestry) alters root exudation and microbial activity, which can modify rhizosphere chemistry. However, direct, high-resolution visualization of the resulting chemical microgradients driving As speciation is lacking, limiting our ability to design cropping systems that mitigate As uptake.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatio-temporal heterogeneity of pH and O2 around roots in cereal-legume-tree alley cropping systems creates distinct 'hotspots' and 'hot moments' for As(III) mobilization, and that this heterogeneity is systematically modulated by plant species diversity and root architecture.
proposals.method: We will employ planar optode sensor films (PreSens, VisiSens TD) to visualize 2D distributions of pH and O2 in rhizoboxes containing As-contaminated soil. The experiment will feature a full factorial design with wheat (Triticum aestivum), faba bean (Vicia faba), and an agroforestry tree (e.g., walnut, Juglans regia) grown in monoculture and intercropped combinations. Time-series images will be captured at key phenological stages. Image analysis will use the manufacturer's software (VisiSens Analytical) for calibration and mapping, with subsequent spatial pattern analysis (e.g., geostatistical variograms) performed in R to quantify gradient extents and heterogeneity linked to root system architecture scans.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially explicit evidence of how plant diversity in complex agroecosystems physically shapes the rhizosphere chemical environment governing As redox. It will generate a novel dataset linking root system architecture to biogeochemical hotspots, advancing the mechanistic understanding of 'multivariate soil health' for contaminant mitigation. The findings will inform the selection of plant combinations in sustainable cropping systems for managing soil pollution risks.
proposals.novelty: While planar optodes have been used in simplified lab studies, their application to decipher the chemical complexity of multi-species agroforestry rhizospheres is novel. The explicit focus on linking visualized O2/pH dynamics to As redox, driven by Hinsinger's expertise in plant-soil interactions in diversified systems, moves beyond descriptive mapping to predictive understanding.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic, Single-Root Model of Arsenic Dynamics in the Rhizosphere: Integrating Speciation, Transport, and Root Exudation for Improved Field-Scale Predictions
proposals.motivation: Arsenic (As) contamination in soils poses a significant risk to crop safety and human health. Field-scale models of As uptake often lack mechanistic detail on rhizosphere processes, leading to poor predictions under diverse soil and cropping conditions. A bottom-up approach, scaling from a single root to the root system, is needed to accurately parameterize the complex interplay of As speciation, transport, and root-induced chemical changes that govern bioavailability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mechanistic model coupling As (oxy)anion transport, redox-driven speciation, and root exudate dynamics at the single-root scale can accurately predict As bioavailability patterns in the rhizosphere, providing essential parameters to improve the accuracy of field-scale crop uptake models.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit, finite-element model using COMSOL Multiphysics to simulate the radial geometry of a single root. The model will integrate: 1) advective-dispersive solute transport of arsenate and arsenite, 2) pH-dependent adsorption/desorption kinetics based on pre-2026 surface complexation models, 3) microbial-mediated redox reactions influenced by simulated root exudates (e.g., organic acids), and 4) root sink terms for As uptake. The model will be parameterized and validated using data from rhizotron experiments with wheat and legume species, measuring As speciation via DGT probes and solution sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, process-based tool to quantify how root-induced biogeochemical gradients control As mobility at the soil-root interface. It will generate essential scaling parameters (e.g., effective uptake coefficients, rhizosphere depletion/enrichment zones) for field-scale models. Ultimately, it will inform strategies for crop selection and intercropping in contaminated soils by elucidating species-specific rhizosphere effects.
proposals.novelty: While root-scale models exist for nutrients like phosphorus, a model explicitly integrating dynamic As speciation shifts driven by root exudation in a spatially resolved rhizosphere is novel. This bridges the gap between complex geochemical speciation codes and practical plant uptake models used in agroecosystem management.

proposals.title: Phosphorus dynamics and bioavailability during early-stage rewilding: linking root trait diversity, soil food web complexity, and phosphatase activity
proposals.motivation: Agricultural intensification has led to widespread soil degradation and simplified ecosystems. In the pre-2026 context of exploring sustainable transitions, intentional land abandonment or rewilding is proposed to restore biodiversity and ecosystem services, yet its impact on biogeochemical cycles, particularly phosphorus (P) availability, remains poorly understood. This knowledge gap hinders the design of resilient agroecosystems that could integrate phases of natural succession.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shift from a managed crop to a diverse plant community during early rewilding will enhance P bioavailability through increased root trait diversity and complexity of the soil food web, leading to greater phosphatase activity and more efficient P cycling compared to a monoculture or bare fallow.
proposals.method: We will establish a replicated field experiment with controlled plots representing a cereal monoculture, intentional abandonment (seed mix of native grasses and legumes), and a natural regeneration treatment. Using minirhizotron imaging and root scanning, we will quantify root architectural and morphological trait diversity. Soil P availability will be tracked via resin membranes and sequential chemical extraction. Phosphomonoesterase and phosphodiesterase activities will be measured fluorometrically. The complexity of the soil food web will be assessed via phospholipid fatty acid (PLFA) analysis and nematode community profiling, with multivariate statistics used to link biological indicators to P dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic understanding of how plant-soil feedbacks drive P cycling in transitioning ecosystems, moving beyond a simple nutrient-stock perspective. It will contribute a novel dataset on P dynamics in rewilding contexts and generate multivariate soil health indicators relevant for assessing ecosystem recovery and designing complex, resilient land-use systems.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the agronomic focus on P management for crop expansion, instead studying P mobilization as a function of intentional ecosystem complexity. It uniquely integrates Hinsinger's expertise in root-soil interactions and soil food webs to address a fundamental biogeochemical question in a novel land-use trajectory.

proposals.title: Rhizosphere Engineering in Alley Cropping: Leveraging Tree-Crop Diversity to Modulate Soil Enzymatic Profiles and Nutrient Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, sustainable intensification requires managing root-soil interactions to enhance nutrient use efficiency and reduce fertilizer dependency. The rhizosphere is a critical yet underexploited interface where plant diversity, particularly in agroforestry systems like alley cropping, can be harnessed to engineer beneficial microbial and biochemical conditions. This project addresses the need for practical strategies to actively manipulate rhizosphere properties for multifunctional agroecosystem benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic selection and spatial arrangement of tree and crop species in alley cropping systems can be designed to create distinct, complementary rhizosphere environments that synergistically enhance the activity of key nutrient-cycling enzymes and improve the acquisition of limiting nutrients (e.g., N, P, K, Mg) beyond what is achieved in monocultures.
proposals.method: The research will combine field experimentation and in-situ rhizosphere sampling. We will establish a replicated alley cropping trial with factorial combinations of tree species (e.g., N-fixing vs. non-fixing) and cereal-legume intercrops. Using soil cores and root ingrowth bags, we will sample rhizosphere soil from different spatial positions (tree row, crop alley, interface). We will quantify multivariate soil health indicators, focusing on enzymatic activities (phosphatase, urease, arylsulfatase), microbial community structure via phospholipid fatty acid analysis (PLFA), and plant nutrient acquisition (N, P, K, S, Mg, Ca) following Hinsinger's established protocols. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (PCA, PERMANOVA) to link rhizosphere properties to plant performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for 'rhizosphere engineering' in complex agroecosystems, demonstrating how planned biodiversity can be used as a tool to manipulate belowground processes. It will deliver actionable design principles for alley cropping systems that optimize rhizosphere functionality for improved nutrient cycling and soil health, contributing to more resilient production systems.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of rhizosphere processes is known, proactively designing plant communities to engineer specific, multifunctional rhizosphere 'types' for agricultural benefit is a novel application. This moves beyond observing diversity effects to actively manipulating them, integrating root ecology, soil biogeochemistry, and agroforestry design.

proposals.title: Inferring Root Activity Profiles in Agroforestry Systems: An Inverse Modeling Approach Using Soil Water and Nutrient Gradients
proposals.motivation: Understanding root activity patterns is crucial for managing belowground competition and complementarity in complex agroecosystems like alley cropping. However, direct measurement of root water and nutrient uptake is destructive and spatially limited. Pre-2026, soil sensors provide high-resolution spatiotemporal data on soil moisture and nutrients, creating an opportunity to infer root function indirectly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatiotemporal gradients of soil water and key nutrients (e.g., K, S, Mg, Ca) measured in the tree-crop interface can be used within an inverse modeling framework to accurately reconstruct the depth- and distance-dependent patterns of root uptake activity for both tree and crop species.
proposals.method: We will establish a field experiment with cereal-legume intercropping in alleys between tree rows, instrumented with soil moisture and multi-ionic sensors (e.g., suction cups, PRS probes). The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian framework, coupling a soil-root process model (e.g., a modified Hydrus-2D/3D) with a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm. This will estimate the posterior probability distributions of root uptake parameters by minimizing the misfit between simulated and observed soil state gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-invasive method to quantify root activity profiles in situ. It will directly contribute to understanding resource partitioning in diversified systems, informing optimal species combinations and spacing in agroforestry for enhanced resilience and nutrient use efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of soil-plant processes is common, the explicit inverse approach to infer root activity from field-measured soil gradients in complex, multi-species agroecosystems is novel. It integrates Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere biogeochemistry with advanced inverse modeling techniques not yet widely applied in this context.

proposals.title: Engineering soil physical structure to direct root system architecture and rhizosphere function in cereal-legume agroforestry systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research emphasizes soil health complexity and plant diversity benefits, but the active management of soil structure as a tool to shape rhizosphere geometry for agronomic gain is underexplored. In alley cropping systems, where tree-crop competition and facilitation occur, strategically constraining root exploration could optimize resource partitioning, enhance beneficial microbial interactions, and improve system resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: That creating defined, compacted soil layers or zones with specific porosity can direct cereal and legume root growth horizontally, reducing direct competition with tree roots and fostering a more stratified, functionally efficient rhizosphere network that enhances nutrient acquisition and soil biological activity.
proposals.method: We will establish a field experiment with an alley cropping system (walnut trees with wheat-faba bean intercropping). Using pre-2026 soil compaction techniques (e.g., controlled tractor passes) and tillage, we will engineer subsoil horizons to create physical barriers. Root system architecture (RSA) will be quantified using minirhizotrons and 3D imaging. Rhizosphere function will be assessed via soil enzymatic activity profiles, PLFA analysis for microbial communities, and isotopic (15N, 13C) tracing of nutrient flows, linking physical structure to biological outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for 'rhizosphere engineering' by demonstrating how managed soil physical constraints can be a lever to optimize root placement and function in complex agroecosystems. It will deliver practical insights for designing alley cropping systems that maximize belowground complementarity and translate multivariate soil health concepts into actionable management.
proposals.novelty: While soil structure is known to influence roots, proactively designing it as a spatial constraint to shape rhizosphere geometry and ecosystem function in multispecies systems is a non-obvious, applied extension of ecological principles, moving beyond observation to active architectural design.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Biotic Interactions in Alley Cropping: A Multivariate Framework to Quantify Neutral and Negative Plant-Soil Feedbacks
proposals.motivation: Agroecological intensification promotes multiple cropping to harness beneficial biotic interactions, but the assumption of net positive effects is oversimplified. Pre-2026 research, including Hinsinger's work on nitrogen and mycorrhizae in intercrops, shows variable outcomes, yet a systematic understanding of when and why interactions become neutral or negative is lacking. This knowledge gap hinders the reliable design of resilient, productive agroecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the net outcome of biotic interactions (e.g., via root exudates, mycorrhizal networks, and soil food web complexity) in alley cropping systems shifts from beneficial to neutral or negative based on specific thresholds of soil nutrient stoichiometry (N:K:Ca:Mg:S) and the functional diversity of the tree-crop combination.
proposals.method: We will establish a replicated field experiment with alley cropping systems (cereal-legume alleys with tree rows), manipulating tree species (functional diversity) and fertilization (N, K, S, Mg, Ca levels based on prior datasets). Using rhizotron and soil coring, we will spatially map root distribution and exudate profiles. Soil health will be assessed via a multivariate indicator suite (PLFA, nematode trophic groups, enzyme activities) to quantify soil food web complexity. Statistical analysis will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) and non-linear regression to identify interaction thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive framework for the context-dependency of biotic interactions, moving beyond the 'always beneficial' paradigm. It will deliver actionable criteria for species and nutrient management to avoid negative plant-soil feedbacks, directly informing the design of sustainable agroecosystems. The multivariate soil health data will advance the functional interpretation of soil complexity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing for negative and neutral interactions as a function of measurable abiotic and biotic drivers, integrating Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere processes and nutrient acquisition with a multivariate soil food web approach. This shifts the research question from 'if' to 'when' interactions fail.

proposals.title: Assessing the robustness and predictive power of a novel rhizosphere biotest across a pedological gradient for agroecosystem multifunctionality
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, soil health assessment is moving beyond single chemical indicators towards integrated, functionally relevant biological assays. A novel rhizosphere biotest, developed in a specific calcareous vineyard soil context, shows promise for quantifying root-induced biological activity. For it to be a widely applicable tool in sustainable agriculture, its performance and interpretation must be validated across diverse soil types with contrasting physicochemical properties, which strongly modulate microbial communities and root-soil interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The functional response measured by the rhizosphere biotest is robust across a wide pedological gradient, and its output can be calibrated against key soil properties and integrated soil health indicators to predict agroecosystem multifunctionality.
proposals.method: We will apply the established biotest protocol (using a standardized plant genotype as a biosensor) to a strategically selected set of 20-30 soils spanning a gradient in texture, pH (acidic to alkaline), organic carbon, and legacy management (from annual cropping to agroforestry systems). Soils will be characterized for standard properties and a suite of pre-2026 multivariate health indicators (e.g., enzyme activities, nematode trophic groups). The biotest output (e.g., root exudation proxies, specific microbial functional guilds) will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (PCA, RDA) and machine learning (Random Forest regression) to identify the dominant soil drivers and build predictive models for nutrient cycling and soil food web complexity metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will determine the operational range and calibration needs of a promising new soil bioassay, moving it from a context-specific tool towards a standardized diagnostic. It will explicitly link root-mediated biological activity, as captured by the biotest, to broader soil health frameworks and cropping system diversity (aligning with Hinsinger's work on plant-soil interactions in complex systems).
proposals.novelty: While biotests exist, systematically evaluating a *rhizosphere*-focused bioassay across a comprehensive pedological gradient to calibrate it for multivariate soil health prediction is novel. It bridges the gap between reductionist plant-level bioassays and field-scale ecosystem multifunctionality.

proposals.title: Spatio-temporal mapping of rhizosphere metal bioavailability in agroforestry systems using whole-cell microbial biosensors
proposals.motivation: In complex agroecosystems like alley cropping, root-induced chemical changes in the rhizosphere critically influence nutrient and contaminant bioavailability, but these processes are dynamic and spatially heterogeneous. Pre-2026 methods (e.g., chemical extractions) fail to capture real-time, micro-scale bioavailability at the soil-root interface, limiting our understanding of plant-soil interactions and the role of plant diversity in modulating rhizosphere chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatio-temporal patterns of bioavailable metals (e.g., Fe, Zn, Cu) in the rhizosphere, as detected by microbial biosensors, will be significantly modulated by plant species identity (cereal vs. legume vs. tree) and intercropping configuration, reflecting distinct root exudation and microbial activity.
proposals.method: We will deploy genetically engineered whole-cell bacterial biosensors (e.g., Pseudomonas fluorescens or Bacillus subtilis strains with metal-responsive promoters fused to gfp/lux reporter genes) in rhizobox experiments. These biosensors will be introduced into the rhizosphere soil of wheat, legume, and tree species grown in monoculture and alley cropping arrangements. High-resolution, real-time luminescence/fluorescence imaging will map metal bioavailability in the thin soil layer adjacent to roots. Data will be correlated with multivariate soil health indicators (enzyme activities, microbial community structure) and plant nutrient acquisition data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in situ, dynamic maps of rhizosphere metal bioavailability in a multispecies cropping system. It will directly link root system diversity to biogeochemical hotspots, advancing the functional understanding of soil health beyond static measures. The dataset will be crucial for modeling nutrient cycling in sustainable agroecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While biosensors exist, their application to map real-time bioavailability in the complex, interacting rhizospheres of diverse crops and trees in a field-relevant agroforestry context is novel. This integrates cutting-edge microbial biotechnology with classic agroecological experimental designs to answer new questions about rhizosphere complexity.

proposals.title: Rhizosphere Engineering in Multi-Species Plant Systems: Competitive Metal Uptake and Soil Modification Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on rhizosphere processes largely focuses on single-species systems, yet agroecosystems are inherently diverse (e.g., intercropping, agroforestry). Understanding how co-cultured species interact belowground to acquire micronutrients (e.g., Fe, Zn) and modify their shared soil environment is critical for designing sustainable, resilient cropping systems that optimize resource use and soil health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in multi-species co-cultures, interspecific root competition will drive species-specific shifts in rhizosphere modification (e.g., pH, exudate profiles), leading to non-additive patterns of metal bioavailability and uptake that cannot be predicted from monoculture performance.
proposals.method: We will modify Hinsinger's biotest (rhizobox) approach to establish controlled co-cultures of species with contrasting nutrient acquisition strategies (e.g., cereal, legume, Brassica). Using isotopic tracers (e.g., 70Zn, 57Fe) and spatially resolved soil sampling, we will quantify competitive metal uptake. Rhizosphere modifications will be tracked via planar optodes for pH/O2 and targeted metabolomics of root exudates. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics and structural equation modeling to disentangle direct and indirect interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for predicting belowground interactions in complex plant communities. It will directly inform the design of intercropping and alley cropping systems for improved micronutrient bioavailability and phytoremediation strategies, contributing to the multivariate understanding of soil health promoted in Hinsinger's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While biotests exist for single plants, scaling this method to dynamically study competitive uptake and real-time, spatially explicit rhizosphere modification in multi-species systems is novel. It bridges the gap between controlled mechanistic studies and the complexity of field-scale agroecosystem diversity.

proposals.title: Rhizosphere Metabolomics in a Rhizobox System: Linking Root Exudate Profiles of Cereal-Legume Intercrops to Cadmium Stress Resilience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, soil contamination by trace metals like cadmium (Cd) threatens sustainable crop production. While plant diversity, particularly intercropping, is known to enhance resilience, the underlying rhizosphere mechanisms, especially the role of species-specific root exudates in modulating metal bioavailability and stress response, remain poorly characterized at the molecular level. A detailed understanding of these exudate profiles is critical for designing cropping systems that leverage plant-soil feedbacks for phytostabilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cereal-legume intercropping, compared to monocultures, will induce a distinct and complementary root exudate metabolome in the rhizosphere, characterized by increased exudation of metal-chelating organic acids and stress-related secondary metabolites, which correlates with reduced Cd bioavailability and improved plant performance under metal stress.
proposals.method: We will use a rhizobox system to non-destructively sample rhizosphere soil from wheat and faba bean grown in monoculture and intercropping under controlled Cd stress. Soil metabolites will be extracted using a methanol:water protocol and analyzed via ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UHPLC-QTOF-MS). Data processing will involve peak picking, alignment, and compound annotation using XCMS and METLIN databases. Multivariate statistical analysis (PLS-DA, PERMANOVA) will link exudate profiles to soil Cd fractions (sequential extraction) and plant physiological data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution metabolomic fingerprint of root exudates from a key agroecological system under metal stress. It will directly link plant diversity to a measurable molecular mechanism in the rhizosphere, contributing to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include biochemical signatures. The dataset will inform the selection of plant species and combinations for phytomanagement of contaminated soils.
proposals.novelty: While Hinsinger's prior work examined nutrient-related enzymes and AMF in diverse systems, this proposal uniquely applies advanced non-targeted metabolomics to the accessible rhizosphere of a defined intercropping biotest, moving beyond bulk soil measures to characterize the specific chemical dialogue that may confer stress resilience.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of rhizosphere chemical gradients: linking root system architecture, plant functional diversity, and soil type
proposals.motivation: The rhizosphere is a dynamic chemical interface critical for nutrient acquisition and soil health, yet its spatial heterogeneity is poorly quantified. Pre-2026 research emphasizes multivariate soil health and plant diversity effects, but lacks high-resolution, species-specific maps of chemical gradients (e.g., pH, phosphatase activity, nutrient depletion) around roots. Such maps are essential to understand belowground complementarity in complex systems like agroforestry and intercropping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometry and intensity of rhizosphere chemical gradients are predictable functions of root system architecture (e.g., root hair density, specific root length) and plant functional type (e.g., legume vs. cereal), and that these patterns are modulated by soil physicochemical properties.
proposals.method: We will use a rhizotron-based approach to grow monocultures and simple mixtures of wheat, faba bean, and an alley cropping tree (e.g., walnut) in contrasting soils (e.g., calcareous vs. acidic). Planar optodes and diffusive gradients in thin films (DGT) will be deployed at key growth stages to image 2D gradients of pH, O2, and labile P/K. Soil zymography will map phosphatase activity. Images will be analyzed using spatial statistics (semivariograms) and gradient geometry will be quantified via custom algorithms in R (e.g., calculating gradient slope and extent from root surface).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, comparative library of rhizosphere gradient geometries for key crop species. It will directly inform the design of root systems for sustainable intercropping by identifying spatial niches for nutrient acquisition. The data will enhance mechanistic models of nutrient cycling in complex agroecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While root exudate sampling exists, simultaneous, high-resolution spatial mapping of multiple chemical variables around diverse, intact root systems in different soils is novel. Linking these gradient maps to root architectural traits and plant functional diversity moves beyond descriptive studies to a predictive framework.

proposals.title: Inferring Root Exudate Composition in Cereal-Legume Agroforestry Systems via Inverse Modeling of Rhizosphere Chemical Gradients
proposals.motivation: Root exudates are central to plant-soil feedbacks, nutrient acquisition, and soil health, yet their in-situ composition remains a 'black box' due to methodological challenges. Direct sampling is destructive and alters exudation patterns. An inverse approach, deducing exudate signatures from the measurable chemical gradients they create, would provide a non-invasive window into rhizosphere processes, crucial for understanding intercropping and agroforestry synergies.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial and temporal gradients of key ions (e.g., H+, HCO3-, carboxylates, NH4+, K+) and pH in the rhizosphere soil solution can be used to inversely model and quantify the composition and flux of major root exudate groups from coexisting plant species.
proposals.method: We will establish rhizobox experiments with wheat, faba bean, and an alley cropping system with a tree species (e.g., walnut). Soil solution will be sampled at high spatial resolution (0-2, 2-5, 5-10 mm from root surface) using micro-suction cups. A process-based geochemical reaction-transport model (e.g., PHREEQC, coupled with COMSOL for 1D transport) will be constructed. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework (e.g., via PyMC3) to estimate the posterior distributions of exudate input rates (organic acids, sugars, protons) that best explain the observed multi-ion chemical gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel methodological framework for non-destructive exudate inference, directly applicable to complex field systems like agroforestry. It will generate quantitative, testable estimates of how exudate profiles differ between species and are modulated by interspecific interactions, linking root functional traits to soil chemistry dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of exudate effects exists, the explicit inverse modeling of exudate composition from in-situ chemical gradients is novel. Combining high-resolution rhizosphere sampling with Bayesian inversion specifically for agroecosystem diversity represents a significant advance over correlative or ex-situ approaches.

proposals.title: Trade-offs between seed nutrient biofortification and plant performance: a trait-based assessment of yield and stress resistance in cereal-legume intercrops
proposals.motivation: Biofortification is a key strategy for addressing human micronutrient deficiencies, yet its potential agronomic costs are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research often focuses on breeding for nutrient density without fully assessing trade-offs with critical agronomic traits like yield and resilience, which are essential for farmer adoption and sustainable food systems. This gap is critical in the context of diversified cropping systems, where plant-plant and plant-soil interactions may modulate these trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cereal and legume varieties bred for high seed concentrations of target micronutrients (e.g., Zn, Fe) will exhibit reduced grain yield and compromised resistance to nutrient or water stress compared to standard varieties, and that these trade-offs are mitigated in cereal-legume intercropping systems through complementary resource use and rhizosphere processes.
proposals.method: The study will use a field experiment with a factorial design, combining (i) crop species (wheat, faba bean), (ii) varietal type (biofortified vs. standard), and (iii) cropping system (monoculture vs. alley cropping intercrop). We will measure agronomic traits (yield, biomass), seed nutrient content, and stress indicators (chlorophyll, canopy temperature). Root system architecture will be characterized using minirhizotron imaging, and rhizosphere processes will be assessed via soil enzyme activities (e.g., phosphatase, β-glucosidase) and multivariate analysis of soil health indicators, linking belowground function to plant performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating the agronomic trade-offs of biofortification, moving beyond a singular focus on nutrient density. It will generate novel insights into how cropping system diversity can buffer potential negative impacts, thereby informing more sustainable breeding and cropping system design. The dataset will contribute to understanding the complexity of soil-plant feedbacks in determining multifunctional agroecosystem performance.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs are suspected, they are rarely tested empirically in the context of diversified agroecosystems. The novelty lies in explicitly linking varietal biofortification traits to whole-plant performance and soil biological function within an intercropping framework, integrating Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere processes and plant-soil interactions.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Benefits of Cereal-Legume Intercropping: A Multi-Site Assessment of Soil Health and Nutrient Acquisition Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Agroecological practices like cereal-legume intercropping are promoted for enhancing nutrient use efficiency and soil health, but their efficacy is often context-specific. Pre-2026 research, including Hinsinger's work, shows that outcomes depend on soil properties and climate, yet most studies are single-site. A systematic, multi-environment assessment is needed to predict where and why these systems succeed, informing resilient agricultural design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the benefits of cereal-legume intercropping on soil enzymatic activities, nutrient acquisition (N, K, S, Mg, Ca), and soil food web complexity will be strongly modulated by inherent soil fertility (e.g., texture, organic matter) and climatic conditions (e.g., aridity), with greater relative benefits in more resource-limited environments.
proposals.method: We will replicate a standardized alley-cropping or tree-intercropping experiment with wheat-faba bean pairs across a network of 8-10 sites spanning major European soil types (e.g., Cambisol, Luvisol, Podzol) and climates (Mediterranean, temperate oceanic, continental). At each site, we will measure: (1) multivariate soil health indicators (enzyme assays, PLFA for microbial community, nematode trophic groups), (2) root distribution and AMF colonization via imaging and microscopy, and (3) plant nutrient acquisition. Treatment effects will be analyzed using linear mixed models with site as a random effect, and the drivers of context-dependency will be identified via redundancy analysis (RDA) linking soil/climate covariates to treatment responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, predictive framework for the context-dependency of intercropping benefits, moving beyond generic recommendations. It will directly contribute to the design of resilient agroecosystems by identifying the soil and climate conditions where diversification yields the greatest gains in nutrient cycling and soil biological complexity.
proposals.novelty: While multi-site trials exist for monocultures, applying a unified, detailed soil health and nutrient acquisition protocol to intercropping systems across environmental gradients is novel. It uniquely integrates Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere processes with a macro-ecological experimental design to disentangle site-specific drivers.

proposals.title: Linking rhizobacterial diversity to root exudate metabolomes and seed nutritional quality in cereal-legume alley cropping systems
proposals.motivation: The nutritional quality of crop seeds is a critical component of food security, yet the rhizosphere mechanisms linking soil biodiversity to seed nutrient content remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Hinsinger and others highlights the importance of soil food web complexity and plant diversity for soil health, but a direct, high-throughput mechanistic link between specific rhizobacterial communities, their induced root exudate profiles, and seed nutrient composition is missing. This gap limits our ability to design agroecosystems that optimize both yield and nutritional outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diversity of rhizobacterial communities in cereal-legume alley cropping systems drives specific shifts in root exudate metabolomes, and that these exudate profiles are predictive of enhanced acquisition and translocation of key micronutrients (e.g., K, S, Mg, Ca) into seeds.
proposals.method: We will establish a controlled experiment with wheat and legume species in rhizoboxes, inoculating with contrasting rhizobacterial consortia isolated from existing alley cropping trials. Root exudates will be collected non-destructively and profiled using untargeted liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) metabolomics. Seed nutrient content will be quantified via ICP-MS. Data integration will use multivariate statistical analysis (e.g., Partial Least Squares Regression) and machine learning (Random Forest regression) to identify exudate metabolites that are both responsive to bacterial diversity and predictive of seed nutrient profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, process-based understanding of how belowground biodiversity influences crop nutritional quality, moving beyond correlative soil health assessments. It will deliver a set of candidate root exudate metabolites that can serve as functional biomarkers for breeding or managing systems for enhanced nutrient density.
proposals.novelty: While root exudate studies exist, the explicit, high-throughput linkage of manipulated rhizobacterial diversity to exudate metabolomes and their functional consequence for seed nutrients in a diversified agroforestry context is novel. This integrates Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere processes, plant nutrition, and complex cropping systems with cutting-edge metabolomics.

proposals.title: A Novel Rhizo-Biotest for Assessing Plant-Soil Feedbacks in Three Dimensions: Moving Beyond the Planar Root Mat
proposals.motivation: Standardized biotests for studying plant-soil interactions, such as the planar root mat method, impose artificial root architectures that poorly represent natural, three-dimensional root growth. This limits our understanding of how root system architecture (RSA) modulates key processes like nutrient acquisition, rhizosphere priming, and microbial recruitment in real soil environments, particularly in complex agroecosystems like alley cropping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a biotest allowing for natural, three-dimensional root growth will reveal stronger and more ecologically relevant plant-soil feedbacks, particularly for nutrient-mobilizing enzymatic activities and mycorrhizal colonization, compared to the traditional planar root mat approach.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular '3D Rhizo-Box' system using thin, soil-filled compartments (e.g., 2 mm thick) arranged in a configurable 3D lattice. This allows roots to grow freely into a defined soil volume while maintaining spatial separation for destructive sampling. The method will be tested using a model cereal-legume intercrop (e.g., wheat-faba bean). We will compare the new 3D system against a classic planar root mat control, measuring root architecture traits via image analysis, quantifying rhizosphere enzyme activities (phosphatase, β-glucosidase), and assessing arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal (AMF) colonization in soil samples taken at different depths and distances from the root base.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for studying root-soil-microbe interactions in a more realistic spatial context. It will directly contribute to understanding how root system complexity influences multivariate soil health indicators, a core theme in sustainable agroecosystem research. The resulting protocol will be applicable for screening crop varietal diversity and intercropping combinations for enhanced rhizosphere functionality.
proposals.novelty: While root observation methods exist, the novelty lies in adapting the standardized, high-throughput biotest format—crucial for controlled experimentation—to accommodate natural 3D root growth, thereby bridging a gap between mechanistic bioassays and field-relevant root architecture.

proposals.title: Optimizing Rhizobox Wicking Solutions for Plant Nutrition with Minimal Soil Chemical Artifacts in Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: Rhizoboxes with wicking systems are vital for studying root-soil interactions in complex systems like agroforestry alley cropping. However, pre-2026 literature highlights that standard nutrient solutions can create significant artifacts by altering soil pH, ionic strength, and nutrient availability, thereby confounding measurements of soil health indicators and root foraging strategies. A standardized, artifact-minimizing wicking solution is needed to improve the ecological realism of controlled experiments on plant diversity and nutrient acquisition.
proposals.hypothesis: A wicking solution with a balanced, low-concentration ionic composition and a pH-buffering capacity matched to the target soil will sustain plant growth while causing significantly fewer chemical disturbances to soil properties (e.g., pH, EC, base cation availability) compared to conventional Hoagland-type solutions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a greenhouse experiment using rhizoboxes filled with soil from a long-term agroforestry trial. Treatments will include a control (no wick), a standard Hoagland solution, and three novel solution formulations varying in total ionic strength, N:K ratio, and buffering agents (e.g., MES). Wheat and a legume (e.g., faba bean) will be grown as model species. We will monitor plant biomass and nutrient status (N, K, S, Mg, Ca) and, critically, measure artifact indicators in the soil: pH, electrical conductivity, enzyme activities (e.g., β-glucosidase, phosphatase), and base cation concentrations at increasing distances from the wick.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, standardized protocol for rhizosphere studies that reduces experimental artifacts, thereby increasing the reliability of data on root processes and soil health in diverse cropping systems. It will directly contribute to methodological advances in studying nutrient cycling in complex agroecosystems, supporting more accurate assessments of sustainability and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While wicking systems are common, the explicit design and testing of solutions to minimize soil chemical artifacts, particularly in the context of multispecies agroforestry systems and in conjunction with multivariate soil health indicators, represents a novel, targeted methodological improvement beyond simply providing plant nutrition.

proposals.title: Synchrotron-based mapping of iron and zinc speciation at the root-soil interface in cereal-legume agroforestry systems
proposals.motivation: The root-soil interface (rhizosphere) is a critical zone for nutrient acquisition, especially for micronutrients like iron and zinc, which are vital for crop nutrition and human health. Pre-2026 knowledge highlights that plant diversity, such as intercropping and agroforestry, can alter rhizosphere chemistry, but the precise spatial distribution and chemical speciation of these metals, which dictate their bioavailability, remain poorly characterized in complex field systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of a legume and/or tree in alley cropping systems will induce distinct, localized changes in the redox state and complexation of iron and zinc at the root mat of a cereal crop, as visualized by synchrotron spectroscopy, thereby creating micro-scale heterogeneity in metal bioavailability.
proposals.method: We will use synchrotron-based micro-X-ray fluorescence (μ-XRF) to map the 2D distribution of Fe and Zn across intact root mat-soil thin sections collected from wheat grown in monoculture versus intercropped with a legume in an alley cropping system. Co-localized micro-X-ray absorption near-edge structure (μ-XANES) spectroscopy will be applied at high-intensity spots to determine the chemical speciation (e.g., Fe(II)/Fe(III), organic complexes, mineral phases). Data analysis will employ linear combination fitting (LCF) of XANES spectra against a library of standard compounds and multivariate statistical analysis (e.g., principal component analysis) to correlate speciation patterns with cropping treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in-situ, spatially resolved speciation maps of key micronutrients at the root mat in a realistic agroforestry context. It will directly link plant diversity to rhizosphere biogeochemistry, offering a mechanistic understanding of how complex cropping systems influence micronutrient cycling. The findings will contribute to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include geochemical parameters.
proposals.novelty: While synchrotron techniques have been used in controlled hydroponic or single-species studies, their application to map metal speciation in the complex, three-dimensional root-soil matrix of a multi-species agroecosystem is novel. This bridges Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere processes with cutting-edge spectroscopic imaging to address a core question in sustainable agroecosystem complexity.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic and functional trade-offs in rhizodeposition: quantifying the carbon cost-benefit for plant fitness across contrasting species in a long-term agroforestry system
proposals.motivation: Rhizodeposition is a major, yet poorly quantified, carbon flux from plants to soil, central to nutrient cycling and soil health. Its evolutionary rationale remains unclear: is it a costly leak or a strategic investment? Pre-2026, most studies are short-term and focus on single species, lacking a phylogenetic framework to assess its survival value across plant lineages with different root strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the carbon 'cost' of rhizodeposition is offset by a measurable long-term 'benefit' in plant fitness, mediated by enhanced soil biotic feedbacks (e.g., mycorrhizal networks, microbial nutrient mobilization), and that this cost-benefit ratio varies systematically with plant phylogenetic lineage and root functional traits.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-existing, long-term Mediterranean agroforestry system (alley cropping with trees and cereals/legumes) as a model. Using 13CO2 pulse-labeling, we will track photosynthate allocation to rhizodeposition in selected species from key lineages (e.g., grasses, legumes, trees). We will concurrently measure plant fitness proxies (biomass, seed yield, perennial survival) and soil feedback mechanisms (enzyme activities, PLFA profiles, mycorrhizal colonization). A multivariate analysis, including structural equation modeling, will quantify relationships between phylogenetic distance, root traits, rhizodeposition flux, soil health indicators, and plant performance.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first empirical, phylogenetically explicit framework for the carbon economics of rhizodeposition. It will directly inform the selection of plant species for designing multifunctional, carbon-efficient agroecosystems that optimize belowground investments for resilience and productivity, bridging evolutionary ecology and applied agroecology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating phylogenetics and long-term agroecosystem context into the carbon budgeting of rhizodeposition, moving beyond single-species, pot-based studies to test its evolutionary adaptive value in a complex, field-based plant-soil system.

proposals.title: Decoupling Mineral and Microbial Drivers of Plant-Soil Specificity in Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: Understanding why certain plant species thrive only on specific soil types is a core challenge in plant ecology and agroecosystem design. Pre-2026 knowledge recognizes that both soil mineralogy and the soil microbiome are key, but their relative contributions and interactions in restricting or facilitating plant establishment remain poorly quantified. This limits our ability to predict plant performance and design resilient, diverse cropping systems that leverage belowground interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that plant species' soil specificity is primarily mediated by a threshold interaction between specific mineral nutrient limitations (e.g., potassium, magnesium) and the composition of key functional groups in the root-associated microbiome (e.g., arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, phosphate-solubilizing bacteria), rather than by either factor alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled rhizobox experiment using a gradient of three contrasting pre-2026 agricultural soils (e.g., calcareous, acidic, alluvial) planted with key crop and tree species from Hinsinger's recent alley cropping research. Using a factorial design manipulating mineral fertilization (based on prior nutrient acquisition datasets) and soil microbial inocula (sterilized vs. live, with specific AMF communities), we will track root development via imaging. Plant performance and soil specificity will be assessed by multivariate analysis of plant biomass, nutrient content, soil enzymatic activities (e.g., N-related), and high-throughput 16S/ITS amplicon sequencing of the rhizosphere microbiome to identify co-varying microbial taxa and mineral factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for predicting plant-soil compatibility by quantifying the relative weights of mineral and microbial drivers. It will directly inform the selection of plant species and soil management practices for complex agroecosystems to enhance sustainability. The results will contribute to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that integrate biotic and abiotic constraints.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies by Hinsinger and others have examined nutrient acquisition and soil food webs separately, this proposal uniquely integrates them in a single experimental framework to decouple their effects, explicitly testing their interaction as the mechanism behind soil restriction. The focus on species used in agroforestry bridges fundamental plant ecology with applied agroecosystem design.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic reconstruction of ancestral rhizosphere traits in cereal-legume intercropping systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that plant phylogenetic history influences root exudation and microbial recruitment, but the evolutionary trajectories of key rhizosphere traits in agroecosystems remain unknown. Understanding these ancestral states is crucial for predicting how plant diversity, particularly in complex systems like alley cropping and cereal-legume intercrops, shapes soil health and nutrient acquisition functions over evolutionary timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ancestral states of key rhizosphere traits related to nitrogen and phosphorus cycling differ significantly between major cereal and legume clades, and that these historical legacies constrain contemporary plant-microbe interactions in diversified cropping systems.
proposals.method: We will compile a pre-existing trait database for 30+ crop and wild relative species from Hinsinger's prior work, focusing on root exudate profiles, mycorrhizal colonization, and phosphatase activity. Using a time-calibrated phylogeny of these species, we will apply Bayesian phylogenetic comparative methods (e.g., MCMCglmm in R) and ancestral state reconstruction algorithms (e.g., maximum likelihood using `ape` and `phytools` packages) to infer trait evolution. Models will account for cropping system (monoculture vs. intercropping) as a discrete environmental variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evolutionary framework for interpreting rhizosphere trait data in agroecology, linking plant diversity to soil function. It will identify which key microbial interaction traits are evolutionarily conserved versus labile, informing the selection of plant species combinations for enhancing soil health and nutrient use efficiency in sustainable cropping systems.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods are standard in ecology, their application to reconstruct the evolutionary history of rhizosphere functional traits in an applied agroecological context, directly integrating cropping system effects, is novel and bridges evolutionary biology with practical soil management.

proposals.title: Spatial Heterogeneity of Potassium Mobility in Soil: Unraveling the Role of Texture and Mineralogy in Creating Functional Niches for Plant Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Potassium (K) is a critical macronutrient for crops, yet its low mobility in soil often limits plant uptake, leading to inefficiencies in fertilization and potential yield gaps. The prevailing view of uniformly poor K mobility overlooks the inherent spatial heterogeneity of soil properties. Understanding how specific combinations of soil texture and mineralogy create localized niches of higher K mobility is essential for developing precision nutrient management strategies that enhance K use efficiency and sustainability in diverse agroecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that soil texture and clay mineralogy interact to create distinct micro-scale niches where K mobility is significantly enhanced compared to the bulk soil matrix, and that these niches can be functionally exploited by specific root architectures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled rhizotron experiment using soils with factorial gradients of texture (sand, silt, clay content) and dominant clay minerals (e.g., illite, smectite, vermiculite). The experiment will feature a cereal-legume alley cropping design, aligning with Hinsinger's recent methods. We will employ non-invasive imaging (e.g., X-ray CT) to quantify root system architecture in situ. Soil solution K dynamics will be monitored at high spatial resolution using micro-suction cups, and K fluxes will be correlated with localized soil properties and root presence. Multivariate analysis of soil health indicators (enzymatic activities, exchangeable K pools) will be integrated to define the functional niches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, spatially explicit framework linking soil physical and mineralogical properties to K bioavailability. It will contribute a novel 'niche-based' perspective to soil K management, moving beyond average bulk soil values. The findings will directly inform the design of cropping systems (e.g., tailored alley cropping, intercropping) and root ideotypes that can better exploit these K-mobile niches.
proposals.novelty: While K fixation is well-studied, the explicit identification and characterization of soil 'niches' with enhanced K mobility as a function of discrete texture-mineralogy combinations is a novel conceptual and empirical advance. Integrating this with root architecture analysis in a complex cropping system directly extends Hinsinger's recent work on multivariate soil health and intercropping nutrient acquisition.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Quantification of Root Exudate Catalytic Efficiency in Mineral Weathering Using Microfluidic Reactor Arrays
proposals.motivation: In agroecosystems, plant acquisition of potassium (K) from non-exchangeable pools in soil minerals is crucial for sustainability, especially in low-input systems. While root exudates are known to enhance mineral weathering, their specific biochemical efficiency remains poorly quantified due to the complexity of the rhizosphere. This gap limits our ability to predict and harness plant-soil interactions for improved nutrient use efficiency in diverse cropping systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific root exudate compounds (e.g., carboxylates, phenolics) exhibit distinct and quantifiable 'catalytic efficiencies' for promoting the dissolution of K-bearing minerals like biotite and K-feldspar, and that these efficiencies can be systematically ranked using a controlled microfluidic platform.
proposals.method: We will fabricate polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-glass microfluidic reactor arrays, each containing micro-channels in contact with thin sections of defined minerals (biotite, microcline). Purified exudate compounds (e.g., citrate, malate, oxalate) at physiological concentrations will be perfused through the arrays at controlled flow rates using syringe pumps. In-situ ion-selective microsensors (K+, H+) and optical pH sensors integrated into the chip will provide real-time, high-resolution data on dissolution kinetics. The experimental design will allow for parallel testing of multiple exudate-mineral combinations under identical hydrodynamic and temperature conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic ranking of root exudate compounds based on their intrinsic capacity to weather K-bearing minerals. It will deliver fundamental parameters (e.g., rate constants) for models of rhizosphere biogeochemistry and inform the selection of plant species or genotypes for enhanced mineral weathering in diversified agroecosystems, such as alley cropping or cereal-legume intercropping.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting high-throughput microfluidic reactor technology, a mainstay of industrial catalysis research, to isolate and quantify the direct biochemical weathering effect of root exudates, moving beyond correlative field studies or bulk solution experiments.

proposals.title: Root Exudate Chemistry and Plant Functional Diversity Drive Potassium Release from Primary Minerals in Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: In the context of sustainable agriculture, reducing reliance on soluble K fertilizers is crucial. Many soils contain K-bearing primary minerals like micas and feldspars, but their dissolution is slow. Root exudates are hypothesized to accelerate this process, yet the quantitative impact of specific exudates from diverse plant species, particularly in complex systems like agroforestry, remains poorly understood, limiting our ability to design systems for enhanced nutrient cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that root exudates (specifically carboxylates and protons) from intercropped cereal and legume species will significantly enhance the dissolution rates of biotite and K-feldspar, and that this effect will be modulated by plant functional diversity and soil microbial communities.
proposals.method: The study will combine controlled hydroponic experiments to collect and characterize root exudates from wheat and legume species with sterile and non-sterile mineral dissolution batch experiments using pure biotite and microcline. Dissolution kinetics will be quantified by measuring K, Si, Al, and cation release via ICP-OES. A complementary field component will employ in-situ root ingrowth cores filled with mineral substrates within an existing cereal-legume-tree alley cropping system, linking exudate profiles (via rhizosphere soil extraction and LC-MS) to mineral weathering signals and plant K acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, species-specific rates of mineral K release driven by root exudates in an agroforestry context. It will generate a functional dataset linking plant diversity, exudate chemistry, and K bioavailability, directly informing the design of resilient cropping systems that optimize natural nutrient pools and reduce fertilizer dependency.
proposals.novelty: While root-induced weathering is known, this study uniquely quantifies the process for K nutrition by integrating controlled mechanistic studies with real-world agroforestry complexity, explicitly testing the interaction between plant diversity and microbial activity on mineral dissolution.

proposals.title: Decoding plant phylogenetic diversity for potassium acquisition strategies: A high-throughput phenomic screen using a defined mineral challenge array
proposals.motivation: Sustainable agroecosystems require reduced reliance on soluble fertilizers by enhancing plant access to soil mineral reserves. Potassium (K) is a key nutrient often locked in silicate minerals, but our understanding of interspecific variation in K acquisition strategies from these sources is fragmented and largely correlative. A systematic, high-throughput approach is needed to functionally link root phenotypes and exudate chemistry to K mining efficiency across diverse plant species.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a phylogenetically diverse plant panel will exhibit discrete, quantifiable K acquisition strategies when challenged with different K-bearing minerals, and that these strategies can be predicted by specific combinations of root architectural traits and root exudate metabolic profiles.
proposals.method: We will construct a 'Potassium Challenge Array' of hydroponic or agar-based growth media, each containing a single, finely-ground, poorly available K source (biotite, illite, K-feldspar, plus a soluble K control and a zero-K treatment). A phylogenetically diverse library of 50-60 plant species (including cereals, legumes, and key agroforestry/tree species) will be grown in this array. High-throughput phenotyping will use automated rhizotron imaging (e.g., RhizoVision) to quantify root hair density, branching, and specific root length. Root exudates will be collected and profiled using untargeted LC-MS metabolomics. Data integration will use multivariate analysis (e.g., PLS-DA) to classify strategies and identify biomarker exudates linked to K uptake from each mineral.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functional classification of plant K acquisition strategies based on direct mineral challenge, moving beyond soil-based studies. It will identify 'mining champion' species for specific mineral types and provide a mechanistic toolkit of root traits and exudate biomarkers. This directly informs the selection of species for intercropping or agroforestry systems designed to access subsoil mineral resources.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the high-throughput screening paradigm from pharmacology to plant-soil interactions, using a defined mineral array to force the expression of specific nutrient acquisition strategies. This enables a shift from observational studies in complex soils to controlled, causal phenomics of a critical nutritional function.

proposals.title: Quantifying subsoil potassium contributions to deep-rooted crops using K stable isotopes and Bayesian mixing models
proposals.motivation: In agroecosystems, subsoil resources are a critical reservoir for crop nutrition, especially potassium (K), but quantifying their direct contribution remains a major challenge. Current methods rely on root observations or total uptake, which cannot partition sources. This limits our ability to design cropping systems (e.g., alley cropping, intercropping) that efficiently mobilize deep nutrients, a key aspect of sustainable soil and nutrient management.
proposals.hypothesis: The stable potassium isotopic composition (δ⁴¹K) of bioavailable soil K differs measurably between topsoil and subsoil horizons, and this distinct isotopic 'fingerprint' can be used to quantify the proportional contribution of subsoil K to plant uptake in deep-rooting species.
proposals.method: We will sample K isotopic signatures from bioavailable (NH₄-acetate extractable) pools in distinct soil horizons (0-30 cm, 30-60 cm, 60-100 cm) in a field experiment with deep-rooting crops (e.g., alfalfa, pigeon pea) and trees. Concurrently, we will collect xylem sap and plant tissues for δ⁴¹K analysis using multi-collector ICP-MS. Source apportionment will be performed using the Bayesian mixing model MixSIAR, adapted from hydrological tracer studies, to calculate the probability distributions of subsoil vs. topsoil K contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first field-based, quantitative method to partition plant K uptake by soil depth, moving from inference to direct measurement. It will generate a novel dataset on K isotope fractionation in agroecosystems and deliver a functional metric for assessing the nutrient foraging efficiency of different plant species and cropping designs.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the well-established hydrological tracer framework to plant nutrition for K, a major nutrient where such isotopic techniques are nascent. It directly bridges isotope geochemistry with agroecology to solve a long-standing quantification problem in plant-soil interactions.

proposals.title: Direct quantification of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal weathering: A compartmented isotopic approach to isolate potassium fluxes from mineral sources
proposals.motivation: The role of arbuscular mycorrhizal (AM) fungi in mobilizing phosphorus from minerals is established, but their direct contribution to potassium (K) weathering remains poorly quantified and is often confounded by root effects. In agroecosystems, K is a critical nutrient, and understanding its biological acquisition pathways is key for developing sustainable nutrient management strategies, especially in low-input or intercropping systems where plant-fungal interactions are complex.
proposals.hypothesis: Extraradical hyphae of AM fungi directly weather K-bearing minerals (e.g., biotite, K-feldspar) through localized proton and chelate efflux, resulting in a measurable and significant K flux into the hyphae, independent of plant root activity.
proposals.method: We will use compartmented rhizoboxes with a root compartment separated by a fine mesh from a hyphal compartment containing ⁴¹K-spiked, defined mineral particles (e.g., biotite). Treatments will include mycorrhizal plants, non-mycorrhizal plants, and mycorrhiza-defective plant mutants (e.g., *Medicago truncatula* mutants). The hyphal compartment will be periodically sampled for soil solution to analyze pH, organic acids, and ⁴¹K enrichment. Hyphal ingrowth cores will be harvested for isotopic analysis to directly quantify K uptake. Specific fungal weathering mechanisms will be probed by adding pH buffers or chelator inhibitors to the hyphal compartment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic proof and quantitative flux data for AM fungal weathering of K minerals, moving beyond correlative field studies. It will delineate the specific contribution of fungi to plant K nutrition in isolation from root effects, refining models of nutrient cycling in complex plant communities.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of compartmented growth systems with isotopic tracing (⁴¹K) and mycorrhiza-defective plant mutants allows for an unprecedented isolation and direct quantification of the fungal weathering flux, a significant advance over previous studies that could not separate fungal and root-derived processes.

proposals.title: Quantifying Potassium Flux Kinetics in Agroecosystems: A Novel Isotopic Tracer Release Experiment in Soil Mesocosms
proposals.motivation: Potassium (K) is a major plant nutrient, but its soil dynamics are poorly quantified, hindering efficient fertilization and understanding of its role in plant nutrition. Current models rely on equilibrium pools rather than kinetic rates of transfer between solution, exchangeable, fixed, and biological pools. This knowledge gap limits our ability to predict K availability in complex, diversified cropping systems, which are critical for sustainable agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing an enriched stable potassium isotope (41K) as a deliberate tracer into the soil solution will allow direct, simultaneous measurement of the kinetic rates of K adsorption, fixation, and plant uptake, and that these rates will be significantly modulated by plant species diversity and root system architecture.
proposals.method: We will adapt the oceanographic tracer release experiment to soil by conducting controlled mesocosm experiments with contrasting plant communities (e.g., monoculture vs. cereal-legume intercropping). A spike of enriched 41K will be injected into the soil solution. Sequential, destructive sampling over time will track the isotopic label into exchangeable (NH4OAc extractable), non-exchangeable (HNO3-extractable), and plant tissue pools. Isotopic ratios will be measured using multi-collector ICP-MS. Data will be fitted to a compartmental model using inverse modeling techniques (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) to derive first-order rate constants for inter-pool transfers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, experimental quantification of kinetic rate constants governing soil K cycling. It will deliver a validated, mechanistic model to predict K dynamics. The results will inform K management in diversified agroecosystems by revealing how plant diversity influences the speed and pathways of K mobilization.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting a deliberate tracer release methodology from oceanography to soil nutrient cycling. Unlike traditional isotopic dilution, it actively labels a specific pool to trace its fate, enabling simultaneous resolution of multiple, competing flux pathways in an intact soil-plant system.

proposals.title: Correlative 3D Imaging Reveals Root Foraging Strategies for Structural Potassium in Soil: A Test with Cereal-Legume Intercrops
proposals.motivation: In agroecosystems, potassium (K) availability is a key yield determinant, yet the mechanistic interactions between roots and K-bearing primary minerals (e.g., K-feldspar, micas) remain poorly visualized. Current methods, like X-ray CT, are chemically blind, while spectroscopic techniques lack 3D spatial context, limiting our understanding of root foraging for mineral nutrients and the role of exudates in mineral weathering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that root system architecture (RSA) of contrasting species (e.g., wheat and faba bean) exhibits distinct spatial patterning and branching behavior in proximity to different K-bearing mineral phases, and that these patterns are modulated by intercropping, reflecting species-specific foraging strategies for structural K.
proposals.method: We will use a custom, co-registered imaging system combining X-ray micro-CT (for 3D RSA) and SWIR hyperspectral imaging (1000-2500 nm) on intact soil cores. Mineral phases (e.g., muscovite, biotite, K-feldspar) will be identified and mapped in 3D using spectral angle mapper (SAM) and mixture-tuned matched filtering (MTMF) algorithms on hyperspectral image cubes. Co-registration will be achieved via fiducial markers and affine transformation in Avizo or Dragonfly software, enabling voxel-level correlation of root proximity with mineral identity and potential alteration features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, 3D visualization of root-mineral interactions for K, quantifying foraging precision. It will generate a novel multivariate dataset linking RSA traits with soil mineralogy, directly informing the complexity of soil-plant feedbacks discussed in soil health frameworks. The results will offer mechanistic insights into the benefits of species diversity for nutrient acquisition in cropping systems.
proposals.novelty: The non-destructive, correlative 3D mapping of specific mineral phases alongside root architecture at the same spatial scale is a significant advance over inferential methods. It moves beyond simple density contrasts to test explicit hypotheses about root behavior in relation to identifiable mineralogical targets.

proposals.title: Direct 4D Visualization of Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Hyphal Networks Mobilizing Potassium from Soil Minerals: Linking Architecture to Function
proposals.motivation: Potassium (K) is a major crop nutrient, yet a large fraction in soil is locked in slowly available mineral forms. The role of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi (AMF) in mobilizing this non-exchangeable K remains poorly understood, despite its critical importance for sustainable nutrient management in low-input and agroforestry systems. Current methods fail to capture the dynamic, in situ interplay between fungal exploration, mineral weathering, and K acquisition in undisturbed soil.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AMF hyphae selectively forage for K-bearing minerals (e.g., micas, K-feldspars) and that the spatial architecture and temporal dynamics of the hyphal network directly govern the rate and magnitude of K mobilization through localized chemical weathering at the hypha-mineral interface.
proposals.method: We will use a novel 4D in situ approach combining synchrotron-based X-ray computed tomography (CT) at high resolution (≤5 µm) with isotopic (⁴¹K) labeling and targeted micro-sampling. Time-lapse CT (4D) of undisturbed soil microcosms containing a K-depleted host plant (e.g., wheat) and defined K-mineral patches will track root and hyphal growth in 3D over time. Post-scan, laser micro-dissection will isolate hyphae in contact with minerals for subsequent NanoSIMS and transcriptomic analysis to quantify K isotopic enrichment and fungal gene expression related to weathering (e.g., proton and organic acid exudation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatio-temporal evidence of AMF-mediated K mining from soil minerals, quantifying the functional contribution of hyphal networks to plant K nutrition. It will generate a mechanistic model linking fungal exploration patterns to biogeochemical weathering processes, advancing our understanding of nutrient acquisition in complex soil environments and informing the design of more resilient cropping systems.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of 4D in situ visualization of hyphal dynamics with targeted geochemical and molecular analysis at the precise interface of interaction moves beyond correlative studies to establish direct cause-effect relationships in an undisturbed context, a significant leap from pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Enzyme-like kinetics of rhizosphere weathering: A modeling framework for predicting potassium and magnesium release from mica and feldspar in agroforestry systems
proposals.motivation: Agroecosystem resilience depends on sustainable nutrient supplies, particularly potassium (K) and magnesium (Mg), which are often limiting in weathered soils. The dissolution of primary minerals like mica and feldspar in the rhizosphere is a critical but poorly quantified source of these nutrients, influenced by complex root-soil-microbe interactions. Pre-2026 soil health frameworks lack mechanistic models to predict this biotically-driven weathering, limiting our ability to design diversified cropping systems that optimize nutrient use efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: The dissolution kinetics of mica and feldspar in the rhizosphere can be effectively modeled using enzyme kinetic frameworks (e.g., Michaelis-Menten), where root exudates (e.g., carboxylates) and microbial metabolites act as 'catalysts', and their concentration and affinity determine mineral dissolution rates.
proposals.method: We will parameterize the model using data from controlled rhizotron experiments with cereal-legume intercrops and tree-based alley cropping systems, measuring dissolution rates of mineral particles in root-ingrowth cores. Exudate composition and concentration will be measured via LC-MS. The core algorithm will adapt the Michaelis-Menten equation, treating exudates as enzymes (E), mineral surfaces as substrates (S), and dissolved ions as products.
