We will test extensions like competitive inhibition by other ions and incorporate spatial heterogeneity using a 2D diffusion-reaction model around root segments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, process-based model to quantify the contribution of rhizosphere biogeochemistry to plant nutrition from soil minerals. It will directly inform the design of diversified cropping systems (e.g., species mixtures, agroforestry) that enhance nutrient acquisition from non-fertilizer sources, contributing to more resilient and sustainable agroecosystem management.
proposals.novelty: While enzyme kinetics are standard in biochemistry, their application to model mineral dissolution as a catalyzed process in the complex rhizosphere environment is novel. This bridges a conceptual gap between soil mineralogy and root physiological ecology, moving beyond descriptive studies to a predictive framework.

proposals.title: Landscape-scale modelling of plant functional diversity effects on potassium dynamics and soil mineral weathering in agroforestry systems
proposals.motivation: Potassium (K) is a critical, yet often limiting, nutrient in agroecosystems, with its long-term availability dependent on the weathering of soil minerals. In the context of sustainable intensification and reduced fertilizer dependency, leveraging plant-soil interactions to enhance K cycling is crucial. However, the scaling of root-mediated K acquisition strategies from individual plants to diverse plant communities at the landscape level remains poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial configuration and functional composition of plant communities (e.g., deep-rooted trees, K-accumulating species, intercropped legumes) in agroforestry landscapes significantly enhance regional K cycling and mineral weathering rates compared to monoculture systems, and that this effect can be predicted by integrating root functional traits and soil geochemistry.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit, process-based model coupling a plant community module (with species-specific root architecture, exudation, and K demand) with a soil geochemistry module (describing K pools and mineral weathering kinetics). The model will be parameterized using pre-2026 trait databases (TRY, FRED) and soil data. We will simulate K dynamics across heterogeneous landscapes using a grid-based architecture, applying the model to virtual and real-world alley cropping designs. Model validation will leverage existing datasets on K acquisition in intercropping systems from Hinsinger's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modelling framework to quantify the 'K mining' service of complex plant communities at a landscape scale. It will deliver actionable insights for designing agroforestry systems that optimize inherent nutrient supplies, contributing to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include biogeochemical cycling functions.
proposals.novelty: While root-scale K acquisition is studied, this project uniquely integrates plant functional diversity, spatial arrangement, and soil mineralogy to model landscape-scale K cycling, moving beyond plot-level studies to inform land-use planning for nutrient resilience.

proposals.title: Comparative Dynamics of Engineered Habitats: Quantifying Rhizosphere Turnover Against Agroforestry and Intercropping Systems
proposals.motivation: The rhizosphere is frequently described as the most dynamic habitat on Earth, a claim central to soil ecology and plant nutrition research. However, this assertion is rarely tested against other biologically engineered, nutrient-rich microhabitats in agroecosystems, such as the detritusphere or the mycorrhizosphere in complex cropping systems. A rigorous, comparative assessment is needed to quantify habitat dynamics and understand the drivers of soil biotic and biogeochemical activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rhizosphere's temporal dynamics of nutrient cycling and microbial community turnover are not uniquely extreme but are comparable to, or even exceeded by, those in other biologically active microsites, specifically the litter-rich soil under tree canopies in alley cropping and the legume-derived detritusphere in cereal-legume intercrops.
proposals.method: We will establish a field experiment comparing wheat monoculture, wheat-faba bean intercropping, and a wheat-allelopathic tree (e.g., *Eucalyptus*) alley cropping system. Using a combination of in-situ soil zymography to map enzyme activity hotspots, sequential coring for high-frequency root and hyphal imaging, and time-series metabarcoding of bacterial and fungal communities, we will quantify key dynamic parameters: reaction rate kinetics, carbon flux, and microbial generation times. Dynamics will be statistically compared using multivariate analyses of time-series data and non-linear modeling of process rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, multi-parameter test of a foundational assumption in rhizosphere science. It will generate a novel dataset on the comparative dynamics of engineered soil habitats, directly informing the design of diversified cropping systems for enhanced soil health and nutrient cycling resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing a long-standing, untested axiom by comparing the rhizosphere to other relevant engineered habitats (detritusphere, alley cropping soil) using a simultaneous, multi-faceted assessment of process rates and community turnover, moving beyond single-system descriptions.

proposals.title: Roots as Net Sources: Quantifying Rhizodeposition-Driven Phosphorus and Nitrogen Mobilization in Cereal-Legume Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: The paradigm of roots as net resource sinks overlooks their potential role in mobilizing poorly available nutrients via rhizodeposition and microbiome interactions. In the context of sustainable intensification and agroecosystem resilience, understanding when and how root systems become net sources for key nutrients like phosphorus and nitrogen is critical for designing diversified cropping systems that enhance internal nutrient cycling and reduce fertilizer dependency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in diversified agroecosystems (cereal-legume-tree alley cropping), the rhizosphere of certain plant species or combinations, through specific root exudates and associated microbial consortia, can become a net source of bioavailable phosphorus and nitrogen for neighboring plants, particularly under low-input conditions.
proposals.method: We will establish a controlled rhizobox experiment with isotopic labeling (15N, 33P) in a soil with low P availability, comparing monocultures of wheat and faba bean with their alley-cropping combination, and including a tree row of *Acacia mangium* for its known rhizosphere acidification. Root exudate profiles will be characterized via LC-MS, while the dynamics of nutrient pools and related enzyme activities (phosphatase, chitinase) will be monitored. The net source/sink function will be quantified by tracking isotopically labeled nutrient flows from donor to receiver plants using a compartmentalized design, coupled with multivariate analysis of soil health indicators and amplicon sequencing of bacterial and fungal communities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing the net resource contribution of roots, moving beyond descriptive exudate studies. It will directly inform the design of species combinations in agroforestry and intercropping that optimize rhizosphere-mediated nutrient acquisition, contributing to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include functional rhizosphere processes.
proposals.novelty: While root exudation is known, systematically quantifying under which species diversity and management conditions the rhizosphere shifts from a net sink to a net *source* of specific nutrients is a novel and functionally significant perspective. It integrates Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere biogeochemistry with contemporary soil food web and multivariate health approaches.

proposals.title: Root-mediated biogeochemical activity as a driver of soil pore architecture in diversified agroecosystems
proposals.motivation: Soil structure is a key determinant of agroecosystem function, but its formation is often viewed as a primarily physical process dictating biogeochemical cycles. This unidirectional view overlooks the potential for biological activity, particularly root exudation and microbial processes, to actively engineer soil architecture. Testing this inverted causality is crucial for understanding how plant diversity, through its influence on rhizosphere biogeochemistry, can build resilient soil habitats.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intensity and diversity of root-mediated biogeochemical activities (e.g., organic acid exudation, enzyme production, gas fluxes) in the rhizosphere of intercropped species will directly and measurably alter local soil pore network geometry and connectivity over a single growing season.
proposals.method: We will establish a controlled rhizotron experiment with monocultures and intercrops of wheat and legume species, aligning with Hinsinger's expertise in alley cropping and root-soil interactions. Using a combination of zymography to map enzyme activity hotspots and planar optodes for pH/O2 dynamics, we will quantify biogeochemical process rates in situ. At sequential time points, the same soil zones will be imaged non-destructively using X-ray computed microtomography (µCT). We will then apply 3D image analysis and pore network modeling to extract metrics of pore architecture (e.g., porosity, pore size distribution, connectivity) and statistically test for causal links between prior biogeochemical activity maps and subsequent architectural changes using structural equation modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental evidence for biogeochemistry as a proximate driver of soil microstructure in living rhizospheres. It will advance the conceptual framework of soil health by explicitly linking plant diversity, multivariate soil biology, and physical habitat formation. The findings will offer a mechanistic basis for designing crop mixtures that optimize soil structure through biological means.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the classical soil physics-first paradigm by treating biogeochemistry as the independent variable and soil architecture as the dependent variable, a direct test of causality rarely attempted. It integrates Hinsinger's multivariate soil health perspective with cutting-edge, spatially explicit correlative imaging of processes and structure in real time.

proposals.title: Bulk Soil Amendments as Levers for Rhizosphere Engineering: Unraveling Indirect Controls on Nutrient Cycling and Microbial Function in Cereal-Legume Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, sustainable intensification aims to reduce fertilizer dependency by optimizing plant-soil-microbe interactions, particularly in the rhizosphere. However, directly manipulating this dynamic zone is impractical at field scales. This proposal addresses the critical knowledge gap of whether strategic bulk soil management can serve as an effective, scalable indirect control for rhizosphere processes, a concept underexplored in complex cropping systems like agroforestry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted bulk soil amendments (e.g., potassium, sulfur, and organic matter) in an alley cropping system will indirectly but predictably alter rhizosphere pH, enzyme activities, and microbial community structure, thereby enhancing nitrogen and phosphorus acquisition efficiency in a cereal-legume intercrop.
proposals.method: We will establish a replicated field experiment with a tree (e.g., walnut)-wheat-faba bean alley cropping system. Treatments will involve factorial applications of potassium sulfate and compost to the bulk soil. Using in-growth mesh bags and rhizoboxes, we will spatially sample the rhizosphere of intercrop species. We will measure multivariate soil health indicators (enzyme activities, PLFA/NLFA for microbial biomass and AMF, pH, nutrient availability) and employ 16S/ITS amplicon sequencing to characterize microbial diversity. Root architecture will be quantified using minirhizotron imaging. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (PERMANOVA, RDA) and structural equation modeling to disentangle direct and indirect causal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for 'rhizosphere engineering via bulk soil,' directly informing management practices for agroforestry and intercropping. It will contribute a novel dataset linking specific bulk amendments to shifts in rhizosphere function and crop nutrient acquisition, advancing the field beyond descriptive studies of soil health to predictive manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While Hinsinger's prior work examines diversity and fertilization effects, this proposal uniquely tests bulk soil as a deliberate manipulation tool to control the rhizosphere, integrating spatial sampling, molecular microbial ecology, and causal modeling in a realistic, multi-species agroecosystem context.

proposals.title: Network Topology of the Rhizosphere Microbiome: Applying Social Network Theory to Predict Multifunctionality in Agroforestry Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, soil health is increasingly defined by the complexity of microbial interactions, not just diversity. However, current microbial ecology lacks robust frameworks to model these interactions as a dynamic system. Social network theory, which quantifies relationships and influence in human societies, offers a powerful, untapped analogy to understand how microbial community structure governs ecosystem functions like nutrient cycling and plant health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key topological metrics from social network analysis (e.g., centrality, modularity, small-worldness) of rhizosphere microbial co-occurrence networks will be stronger predictors of soil multifunctionality (e.g., enzyme activities, nutrient supply) than traditional taxonomic diversity indices, and that these network properties are modulated by plant species diversity in alley cropping systems.
proposals.method: We will sample rhizosphere soil from wheat-legume intercrops within a mature Mediterranean alley cropping system (aligning with Hinsinger's recent work). Microbial communities will be characterized via 16S and ITS amplicon sequencing. Co-occurrence networks will be constructed using the SPIEC-EASI algorithm, which uses graphical lasso to infer robust, sparse microbial associations. Resulting networks will be analyzed in R (`igraph`) to calculate social network metrics: degree/betweenness centrality (identifying 'keystone' taxa), clustering coefficient, and modularity. These metrics will be statistically related to a multivariate soil health index (encompassing enzymatic activities and nutrient acquisition data) using redundancy analysis and structural equation modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-level framework for diagnosing soil health based on microbial interaction architecture. It will directly test the theoretical link between network complexity and ecosystem resilience proposed in recent soil food web research. The findings will offer actionable insights for designing cropping systems that foster beneficial microbial network topologies.
proposals.novelty: While microbial networks are commonly constructed, the explicit application of social network theory metrics and hypotheses (e.g., identifying 'influencer' taxa via centrality) to predict agroecosystem function is novel. This cross-disciplinary approach moves beyond description to mechanistic, predictive modeling of the rhizosphere social network.

proposals.title: Mapping Landscape-Scale Rhizosphere Function: Integrating Root Architecture, Soil Biogeochemistry, and Plant Diversity to Predict Nutrient Fluxes
proposals.motivation: Current soil health assessments often focus on bulk soil properties, failing to capture the spatial heterogeneity and biological complexity of the rhizosphere, the critical interface for plant resource acquisition. Scaling rhizosphere processes from individual roots to fields or landscapes is a major knowledge gap, limiting our ability to predict nutrient fluxes, identify localized deficiencies, and manage agroecosystem resilience under changing conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial aggregation of key rhizosphere properties (e.g., phosphatase activity, mycorrhizal colonization, pH gradients) can be modeled using plant functional diversity, root system architecture, and soil type to generate predictive maps of nutrient acquisition potential and limitations at the landscape scale.
proposals.method: We will establish a network of sampling points across a landscape gradient incorporating alley cropping systems and cereal-legume intercrops. At each point, we will characterize root architecture (via minirhizotron imaging and root scanning), rhizosphere enzyme activities, AMF colonization, and nutrient concentrations. These point data will be integrated with high-resolution remote sensing (multispectral UAV) and soil electrical conductivity maps. We will employ geostatistical kriging and machine learning algorithms (specifically Random Forest regression) to interpolate and model rhizosphere properties, using plant diversity, soil type, and spectral indices as predictors to create continuous landscape maps of resource fluxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit maps of rhizosphere function, moving beyond point measurements to a systemic view of belowground resource dynamics. It will provide a novel framework for diagnosing landscape-scale nutrient limitations and inform precision management strategies that leverage plant diversity to enhance soil health and agroecosystem sustainability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit spatial scaling of multivariate rhizosphere processes by integrating Hinsinger's expertise in rhizosphere biogeochemistry with geostatistics and remote sensing, shifting the paradigm from describing single-root effects to predicting emergent landscape-level functionality.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Root Ideotypes to Engineer Rhizosphere Functionality in Degraded Soils
proposals.motivation: Current soil restoration strategies focus on amending soil directly, which can be costly and unsustainable. A more resilient approach, aligned with agroecological principles, would be to leverage plants as ecosystem engineers. This proposal addresses the inverse problem: defining the optimal root traits for a plant to create a target, multifunctionally healthy rhizosphere in a given degraded soil, thereby enhancing soil health and crop performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given soil type and a defined target rhizosphere state (e.g., specific enzyme activity profiles, microbial functional diversity), a unique combination of root architectural, exudative, and morphological traits can be computationally identified and then validated through plant breeding or selection.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational framework. First, a forward model will be built using a Random Forest algorithm trained on a pre-existing multivariate dataset linking measured root traits (architecture, exudate profiles) to multivariate soil health indicators (enzyme activities, PLFA, soil structure) from diverse crop species in field trials. Second, an inverse model will be solved using a Bayesian optimization algorithm to identify the combination of root trait values that maximizes the probability of achieving the target soil state. Candidate ideotypes will be tested in controlled rhizobox experiments with wheat and legume genotypes varying in key root traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, trait-based framework for designing cropping systems that actively engineer soil health. It will move the field from descriptive correlations between roots and soil to a predictive, design-oriented science. The resulting 'root ideotype' blueprints will directly inform breeding programs for crops tailored to soil restoration in alley cropping and intercropping systems.
proposals.novelty: While much research correlates root traits with soil properties, the explicit inverse problem—designing roots for a soil outcome—is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in combining multivariate soil health assessment with machine learning inversion to generate testable root ideotypes, shifting the paradigm from plant adaptation to plant-driven soil design.

proposals.title: Coupling the Jena Soil Model with a Dynamic Plant Growth Framework to Simulate Plant-Soil Feedbacks in Diverse Cropping Systems
proposals.motivation: Current soil models (e.g., JSM) and plant growth models largely operate in isolation, limiting our ability to mechanistically predict agroecosystem resilience and nutrient use efficiency. Closing the plant-soil feedback loop is critical for designing sustainable, diversified cropping systems (e.g., alley cropping, intercropping) that optimize belowground interactions. This integration is needed to move beyond static soil health indicators and simulate the dynamic, species-specific feedbacks that govern soil food web complexity and nutrient cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a full two-way coupling of the Jena Soil Model (JSM) with a dynamic plant growth model will significantly improve the simulation of nitrogen and phosphorus dynamics, root exudation patterns, and microbial activity in cereal-legume intercrops compared to uncoupled or one-way linked models.
proposals.method: We will couple the process-based JSM, which simulates C, N, P, and S cycling with explicit microbial functional groups, with the ArchiSimple root architecture model and a crop-specific growth module. The coupling will be implemented via a shared numerical framework (e.g., using the RECO model coupling library) to enable daily exchange of state variables: the plant module provides root exudates, water/nutrient uptake, and litter input, while JSM returns soil nutrient concentrations and microbial biomass. The integrated model will be parameterized and validated using existing datasets from Hinsinger's intercropping and alley cropping trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully coupled JSM-plant model, providing a novel tool to mechanistically explore how plant diversity (species, cultivars) shapes soil biogeochemistry and vice-versa. It will advance the theoretical framework for plant-soil feedbacks by quantifying the role of root architectural plasticity and exudation in modulating soil health indicators and nutrient acquisition in complex agroecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While soil and plant models have been linked before, a full bidirectional coupling of a complex soil food web model like JSM with a dynamic root architecture model is novel. This approach uniquely captures how plant phenotypic traits feed back on microbial community functions and nutrient mineralization rates in real-time.

proposals.title: Game-Theoretic Modeling of Nutrient Acquisition Strategies in Cereal-Legume Intercrops: Integrating Root Architecture and Microbial Mediation
proposals.motivation: Optimizing nutrient use efficiency in agroecosystems, particularly in diversified systems like intercropping, is crucial for sustainable intensification. Pre-2026 research by Hinsinger and others shows complex, context-dependent interactions between plant roots and soil microbes (e.g., mycorrhizal fungi) for nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus, but a unifying theoretical framework to predict outcomes is lacking. Framing these interactions as a game can formalize the trade-offs between competitive and cooperative strategies, moving beyond descriptive studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nutrient acquisition strategies of intercropped cereal and legume plants, mediated by root exudation and associated microbial communities, can be accurately modeled as a non-cooperative game where Nash equilibria correspond to observed root foraging patterns and nutrient uptake efficiencies.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model where plant functional types (cereal, legume) and a mycorrhizal fungal agent are players in a spatial game on a 2D soil grid. Players' strategies involve carbon allocation to root growth/exudation versus shoot, with payoffs defined as acquired N/P per carbon invested. The model will be parameterized with pre-2026 data from Hinsinger's intercropping experiments. We will use iterative best-response algorithms to solve for Nash equilibria under different soil nutrient heterogeneities and compare model outputs to empirical data on root distribution and enzyme activities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework to predict nutrient partitioning in multispecies cropping systems, linking root ecology with game theory. It will generate testable predictions on how plant-soil feedbacks and initial resource states determine the stability of cooperative nutrient acquisition. The model will be a tool for designing intercropping systems that optimize resource complementarity.
proposals.novelty: While game theory has been applied to plant competition for light, its application to belowground nutrient acquisition, explicitly integrating microbial mediators as strategic players, is novel. This moves beyond traditional resource ratio models by incorporating strategic decision-making and adaptive behaviors.

proposals.title: Linking Root System Architecture to Microscale Weathering Signatures: A 4D X-ray CT and Geochemical Tomography Approach in Agroforestry Soils
proposals.motivation: Understanding how plant diversity shapes soil biogeochemistry is central to sustainable agroecosystem design, yet we lack mechanistic insight into how specific root systems physically and chemically engineer their immediate soil microsites. Pre-2026 research, including Hinsinger's work on intercropping and nutrient acquisition, highlights the importance of root-soil interactions but relies on bulk measurements that average across heterogeneous hotspots. Directly linking the 3D physical architecture of roots from different species to the geochemical fingerprints they generate at the mineral interface remains a critical, unresolved challenge in rhizosphere science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct root system architectures (e.g., from cereal vs. legume vs. tree species in an alley cropping system) and their associated microbial communities create spatially unique and diagnosable geochemical weathering signatures in the surrounding soil matrix, which can be resolved by correlating in situ structural imaging with micro-sampled porewater chemistry.
proposals.method: We will use time-series, non-destructive X-ray computed tomography (CT) at synchrotron or high-resolution lab facilities to image the 4D development of root systems of contrasting species (e.g., wheat, faba bean, walnut) interacting with mineral grains in custom mesocosms. This 3D structural data will guide the precise, spatially targeted extraction of porewater from distinct weathering microsites (e.g., root tips, root hairs, mycorrhizal hyphae zones) using micro-suction rhizon samplers. The fluid will be analyzed via ICP-MS and ion chromatography for a multi-elemental and anionic signature. Correlation will be achieved through 3D image registration and geostatistical analysis (e.g., kriging) to map chemistry onto physical structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially explicit evidence linking biotic engineering to unique chemical weathering patterns, moving beyond bulk soil measurements. It will advance the conceptual framework of soil health by defining a 'biotic fingerprint' of nutrient mobilization. The methodology will establish a novel protocol for spatially resolved rhizosphere studies with applications in agroecology and astrobiology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, spatially resolved fusion of 4D root architecture imaging with geochemical tomography at the same microsites, a technical synergy not previously applied to agroforestry systems. This moves from observing correlations to establishing causative links between specific biotic 'engineers' and their diagnostic chemical signatures in undisturbed soil.

proposals.title: Dynamic nutrient and water regimes drive rhizosphere weathering: a mesocosm study with cereal-legume-tree alley cropping
proposals.motivation: Soil mineral weathering is a critical, slow-release source of nutrients (e.g., K, Mg, Ca) for sustainable agroecosystems, but its study is often confined to controlled, static laboratory conditions. In field-realistic settings, nutrient and water availability fluctuate due to management, plant uptake, and climate, directly influencing root-induced weathering processes. Understanding these dynamics is essential for designing resilient, low-input cropping systems that optimize natural nutrient cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluctuating, field-realistic regimes of nutrient and water availability, as opposed to constant optimal conditions, will significantly alter the rate and stoichiometry of rhizosphere weathering by modulating root exudation and microbial activity, with the effect being amplified in functionally diverse plant communities.
proposals.method: We will establish a replicated mesocosm experiment mimicking an alley cropping system, with columns containing a defined, low-fertility soil developed from a known mineral substrate (e.g., biotite-bearing soil). Treatments will combine (i) plant diversity (wheat monoculture, wheat-faba bean intercrop, + tree seedling rows) with (ii) irrigation/nutrient regimes (constant optimal vs. fluctuating drought and nutrient pulses). Weathering will be tracked via periodic leachate analysis for base cations and silicon, and destructive sampling for soil solution chemistry, exchangeable cations, and root exudate profiling. Multivariate analysis of soil health indicators (enzymatic activities, microbial biomass) will link biological processes to weathering outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based understanding of how real-world environmental variability governs a key ecosystem service—soil nutrient supply. It will generate a unique dataset on plant acquisition of rock-derived nutrients under dynamic conditions, directly informing the management of complex agroecosystems like alley cropping for enhanced resource use efficiency and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While Hinsinger's prior work examines nutrient acquisition in intercrops, this proposal explicitly shifts the focus from static nutrient availability to dynamic regimes as the driver of weathering, integrating this physical-chemical process with the complexity of soil food webs and multivariate soil health in a designed multi-species system.

proposals.title: Disentangling the contributions of roots, mycorrhizal fungi, and free-living microbes to mineral weathering in a tree-based intercropping system
proposals.motivation: Agroforestry systems enhance soil mineral weathering, a key process for long-term nutrient sustainability, but the specific, quantitative contributions of different biotic components (plant roots, mycorrhizal fungi, and soil microbes) remain entangled. Understanding these individual contributions is critical for designing cropping systems that optimize the biological enhancement of soil nutrient pools, a core goal for reducing reliance on mineral fertilizers in sustainable agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a cereal-tree alley cropping system, (1) the weathering effect of plant roots is primarily physical and acid-based, (2) arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi significantly enhance weathering through hyphal exploration and chelation, and (3) the rhizosphere microbial community contributes substantially via organic acid exudation, with these effects being non-additive when components interact.
proposals.method: We will establish a controlled pot experiment using a compartmented 'root-mesh-hyphae' design with a model cereal (wheat) and a legume tree (e.g., *Leucaena*). Treatments will systematically exclude or allow roots, AMF hyphae (using mesh barriers of specific pore sizes), and the bulk soil microbiome (via sterilization and re-inoculation). Weathering of a standardized mineral substrate (e.g., biotite or ground basalt) will be quantified by measuring the release of potassium, magnesium, and silicon into leachates over a growing season. Soil solution chemistry, microbial community structure (via 16S/ITS amplicon sequencing), and root exudate profiles will be analyzed to link mechanisms to flux measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative partitioning of weathering fluxes among key biotic drivers in a managed agroecosystem context. It will directly inform the selection of plant species and management practices in agroforestry that maximize biological weathering, contributing to the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include specific biological process rates.
proposals.novelty: While plant- and microbe-mediated weathering is known, the systematic decoupling of these components in a realistic, multi-species cropping context—bridging Hinsinger's work on rhizosphere processes and agroecosystem complexity—is a novel experimental approach to move beyond correlative studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying rhizosphere biogeochemical dynamics in a soil-realistic 3D microfluidic mesocosm: linking root exudation, microbial hotspots, and mineral weathering
proposals.motivation: The rhizosphere is a dynamic, spatially heterogeneous interface where root exudates, microbes, and soil minerals interact to drive nutrient cycling. However, our mechanistic understanding is limited by the opacity of soil and the oversimplified geometries of traditional 2D microfluidic devices, which fail to capture the complex pore networks and interfaces of real soil. A more realistic, observable model system is needed to bridge the gap between field studies and controlled experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 3D architecture of soil pores, replicated from micro-CT scans, will create spatially constrained microbial hotspots and chemical gradients that significantly alter the rates and patterns of root-driven mineral weathering and nutrient mobilization compared to simplified 2D systems.
proposals.method: We will fabricate polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based microfluidic mesocosms with 3D-printed scaffolds whose geometries are derived from micro-CT scans of undisturbed soil cores from Hinsinger's previous field sites. The devices will incorporate embedded microsensors (e.g., for pH, O2, NO3-) and transparent observation windows. A model cereal (wheat) and legume (medic) will be grown individually and in intercropping configurations. We will use confocal microscopy and fluorescent probes to visualize root exudate diffusion, bacterial/fungal colonization (using GFP/RFP-tagged strains), and mineral dissolution patterns on incorporated mineral particles (e.g., K-feldspar, apatite).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first real-time, spatially explicit data on how soil physical complexity modulates rhizosphere processes. It will directly test the impact of 3D architecture on nutrient acquisition efficiency, offering a mechanistic basis for interpreting field observations on intercropping and plant diversity effects. The developed platform will serve as a new standard for ecologically valid rhizosphere simulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of *in-situ* soil microstructure into a functional, observable experimental platform, moving beyond idealized 2D pore networks. The concurrent, multi-parameter sensing within this realistic matrix for intercropped species is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Rhizosphere Mimics Reveal the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Mineral Dissolution and Microbial Colonization in Cereal-Legume Intercropping Systems
proposals.motivation: The rhizosphere is a dynamic hotspot for nutrient mobilization, yet its complexity and opacity make real-time, mechanistic observation of processes like mineral dissolution and root-microbe interactions extremely challenging. This limits our ability to understand and manage nutrient acquisition in diversified cropping systems, such as cereal-legume intercrops, which are promoted for sustainable agroecosystem resilience. Pre-2026 microfluidic 'lab-on-a-chip' technology from chemistry and microbiology offers a transformative tool to visualize these hidden processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the root exudate profiles from intercropped wheat and faba bean, when simulated in a micromodel, will create distinct, synergistic patterns of mineral (K, Mg, Ca-bearing) dissolution and selective microbial biofilm formation that differ from those observed under monoculture conditions.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-glass microfluidic devices containing micro-patterned chambers filled with synthetic or natural soil mineral particles (e.g., biotite, dolomite). Using micro-patterning and soft lithography, we will create root-like channels. These will be perfused with synthetic exudate solutions derived from monocropped and intercropped wheat and faba bean, based on prior metabolomic data. Real-time dissolution will be monitored via integrated pH and ion-selective sensors and time-lapse microscopy. We will subsequently inoculate the devices with a defined microbial community (including bacteria and arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi spores) to visualize colonization patterns using fluorescent tags.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, visual evidence of how plant diversity, mediated by root exudates, orchestrates rhizosphere biogeochemistry and microbiology at the micro-scale. It will generate a mechanistic dataset linking exudate chemistry to mineral weathering and microbial habitat formation, directly informing the development of multivariate soil health indicators. The findings will advance the theoretical framework for sustainable agroecosystem design by uncovering the micro-scale drivers of nutrient acquisition in complex systems.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used to study bacterial motility or pure mineral dissolution, its application to create a controlled, multi-component 'micromodel rhizosphere' that integrates plant diversity (intercropping exudates), soil minerals, and a microbial community is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk soil measurements or simplified hydroponics to a spatially explicit, process-based platform.

proposals.title: Direct Visualization of Microscale Weathering: Quantifying the Kinetics and Mechanisms of Single Root Hair and Mycorrhizal Hypha Interactions with Biotite Surfaces
proposals.motivation: While plant and microbial contributions to mineral weathering are recognized as key drivers of nutrient cycling and soil formation, the fundamental mechanisms at the organism-mineral interface remain a 'black box'. Current soil health and agroecosystem resilience frameworks, including those emphasizing soil food web complexity, lack mechanistic understanding of these primary weathering events, which govern the release of potassium, magnesium, and iron.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the weathering kinetics and mechanism (e.g., ligand-promoted vs. proton-promoted dissolution) differ fundamentally between a single root hair of a cereal crop and a single arbuscular mycorrhizal (AM) fungal hypha when interacting with the same biotite grain, driven by distinct exudate chemistries and physical contact modes.
proposals.method: We will use a microfluidic 'mineral sandwich' device to trap a single, characterized biotite flake. A single root hair from a wheat seedling (grown axenically or in association with the AM fungus *Rhizophagus irregularis*) or a single AM fungal hypha (from in vitro cultures) will be guided into contact with the biotite surface using micromanipulation. Real-time, in situ observations will be conducted using vertical scanning interferometry (VSI) to quantify nanometer-scale surface dissolution topography. Exudates from the single hair/hypha will be sampled via micro-capillaries for subsequent HPLC-MS analysis to correlate dissolution patterns with organic acid and phytosiderophore profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative kinetics of weathering by individual soil biota, moving beyond bulk measurements. It will establish a causal link between specific exudate signatures and dissolution mechanisms, offering a process-based foundation to scale from single grains to field-level nutrient availability predictions in diverse cropping systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in isolating and measuring the action of a single, living biological actor on a single mineral surface in real time, an experimental scale not yet achieved in soil biogeochemistry, which typically relies on bulk root systems or microbial consortia.

proposals.title: Disentangling Soil Fauna Guild Contributions to Mineral Weathering: A Trophic Network Fractionation Approach
proposals.motivation: Soil fauna are recognized as key biotic drivers of mineral weathering, a fundamental process for long-term soil fertility and carbon sequestration. However, the specific and interactive contributions of different functional guilds (e.g., microbivores, detritivores, predators) remain a 'black box', limiting our ability to manage soil health and biogeochemical cycles in agroecosystems. This knowledge gap mirrors historical challenges in marine biogeochemistry, where the roles of different microbial trophic levels in nutrient cycling were similarly conflated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct soil fauna guilds differentially modulate silicate mineral weathering rates through specific direct (e.g., physical comminution) and indirect (e.g., trophic cascades altering microbial community function) pathways, and that their net effect is non-additive.
proposals.method: We will adapt marine size-fractionation and selective inhibition techniques to soil mesocosms containing a standardized silicate mineral (e.g., biotite). Using a factorial design, we will sequentially manipulate guilds via physical sieving, selective biocides (e.g., fungicides, bactericides, nematicides), and staged introductions of fauna (nematodes, collembola, mites). Treatments will isolate microbivore, detritivore, and predator effects. Weathering will be tracked via solution chemistry (K, Mg, Si, pH) and mineral surface analysis (SEM, XRD). Soil microbial community structure and enzyme activities will be monitored to quantify indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative partitioning of weathering fluxes attributable to specific soil food web compartments. It will generate a mechanistic framework linking soil biodiversity complexity to weathering dynamics, directly informing the development of multivariate soil health indicators that include faunal functions. The dataset will advance predictive models of nutrient cycling in complex cropping systems like agroforestry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit translation of a marine biogeochemical experimental paradigm (trophic-level fractionation) to soil ecology, enabling the disentanglement of entangled biotic interactions in weathering. This guild-level manipulation moves beyond correlative studies or whole-fauna additions to establish causal, partitioned effects.

ideas.title: In situ 4D visualization of mycorrhiza-mediated potassium mobilization from soil minerals
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution X-ray computed tomography (CT) with isotopic labeling and targeted sampling to visualize and quantify the process of potassium mobilization in real-time. It will track the growth of roots and mycorrhizal hyphae in 3D as they interact with specific soil minerals known as K sources, using time-lapse imaging (4D) to capture dynamics. By combining the spatial architecture from CT with geochemical and molecular analysis of the hyphae-mineral interface, the study will directly link fungal exploration strategies to the measured rates of K release and uptake. This approach reveals the mechanistic sequence of physical contact, biofilm formation, and chemical weathering driven by the symbiotic network.

ideas.title: Quantifying the direct mycorrhizal weathering flux: Isolating fungal mechanisms for potassium acquisition from minerals
ideas.description: This research employs compartmented growth systems and isotopic tracers to isolate and quantify the direct contribution of extraradical mycorrhizal hyphae to potassium (K) mobilization from mineral particles, independent of plant roots. It will compare K fluxes from defined mineral sources in hyphae-only compartments to systems with non-mycorrhizal plants and mycorrhiza-defective mutants. The experimental design will identify specific fungal weathering mechanisms, such as proton efflux or chelate production, and link them to K acquisition rates. This approach definitively tests the hypothesis of direct fungal weathering, moving beyond correlative observations to mechanistic proof.

ideas.title: Correlative X-ray CT and SWIR Hyperspectral Imaging to Link Root Architecture with Potassium Mineral Speciation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the chemical blindness of standard CT by integrating it with SWIR hyperspectral imaging to non-destructively map both root system architecture and the spatial distribution of specific K-bearing mineral phases in soil. The co-registered 3D datasets allow for the quantification of root growth patterns and branching in direct relation to identifiable sources of structural potassium, such as K-feldspar or micas. This enables the direct testing of hypotheses about root foraging behavior for different mineral types and the visualization of localized mineral alteration features potentially induced by root exudates. The system provides a robust, correlative platform for studying the mechanistic interactions between roots and soil mineralogy, rather than inferring them from density contrasts alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Oceanographic Tracer Release Experiments to Soil Potassium Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from chemical oceanography, where deliberate tracer release experiments are used to quantify fluid dynamics, to the problem of tracing potassium fluxes in soil. The analogous solution involves releasing an inert chemical tracer (like SF6) to label and track a water mass. This is adapted by conducting a controlled soil mesocosm experiment where a spike of an enriched stable potassium isotope (41K) is introduced into a specific initial pool (e.g., the soil solution). By meticulously tracking the dispersion of this isotopic label into exchangeable, mineral, and biological pools over time using multi-collector ICP-MS, the method can directly measure the kinetic rates of adsorption, fixation, and plant uptake, providing a powerful tool to construct and validate quantitative models of soil potassium cycling.

ideas.title: Assess subsoil K contribution using isotopic tracer techniques adapted from hydrology
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from hydrology to solve the analogous problem of quantifying source contributions in plant nutrition. In hydrology, stable water isotopes are used in mixing models to determine the proportion of water from different sources (e.g., groundwater, rainfall) in a stream or plant. The analogous solution is adapted by using the natural stable isotopes of potassium (⁴¹K and ³⁹K), which can exhibit measurable fractionation and distinct signatures in different soil layers due to weathering and biological processes. The research idea is to systematically sample the K isotopic signature ('fingerprint') of bioavailable K in topsoil and subsoil horizons, and compare it to the isotopic signature found in the xylem sap or harvested tissues of deep-rooting crops. Applying a Bayesian mixing model will then allow for a quantitative, field-based assessment of the proportion of plant K derived from subsoil foraging, moving beyond inference based on root presence or total uptake measurements.

ideas.title: Spatially Resolving Biotic Fingerprints in Weathering Microsites via In Situ X-ray CT and Geochemical Tomography
ideas.description: This research integrates non-destructive, time-resolved X-ray computed tomography (CT) of root systems interacting with soil mineral grains with high-resolution, spatially targeted sampling and analysis of the resulting porewater chemistry. By correlating the 3D physical structure of distinct root types (e.g., from different plant species or microbial associations) with the geochemical fingerprints they generate in micro-scale weathering hotspots, the study directly tests whether unique biotic 'engineers' produce diagnostic chemical signatures. This approach moves beyond bulk solution analysis to determine if biosignature uniqueness is preserved or homogenized at the critical interface where biology actively alters minerals, fundamentally advancing the search for life's traces in terrestrial and planetary regolith.

ideas.title: A High-Throughput Phenomic Screen for Plant Potassium Acquisition Strategies Using a Defined Mineral Challenge Array
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput screening paradigm from antimicrobial drug discovery to plant nutrition research. The analogous solution involves screening chemical libraries against a bacterial strain panel to find effective compounds. Adapted here, we propose constructing a defined 'Potassium Challenge Array'—a series of growth media each with a different, poorly available potassium source (e.g., biotite, illite, K-feldspar). A phylogenetically diverse library of plant species would be systematically grown on this array. Using automated phenotyping (e.g., rhizotron imaging for root hairs, branching) and metabolomic profiling of root exudates, we can perform a high-throughput 'phenomic screen.' This approach will efficiently classify K acquisition strategies, identify species with superior mining capabilities for specific mineral types, and directly link those capabilities to quantifiable root traits and exudate biomarkers, moving beyond correlation to causal understanding of strategy efficiency.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Catalytic Efficiency of Root Exudates in Mineral Weathering Using Microfluidic Reactor Arrays
ideas.description: This research adapts high-throughput screening methods from industrial catalysis to quantify the impact of specific root exudates on mineral dissolution. The analogous problem in chemical engineering—rapidly testing catalyst performance—is solved using microfluidic reactor arrays with integrated sensors under controlled conditions. The adapted idea involves fabricating microfluidic devices ('mineral weathering chips') containing thin sections of micas or feldspars. By perfusing these chips with purified root exudates at controlled flow rates and concentrations, and using in-situ sensors to monitor ion release (e.g., K⁺) and pH changes, the research will precisely measure and compare the dissolution-enhancing 'catalytic efficiency' of different exudate compounds. This platform isolates the chelating and acidifying effects of exudates from other soil processes, providing quantitative, mechanistic data on how root biochemistry directly accelerates potassium weathering from soil minerals.

ideas.title: Trophic Network Engineering: Manipulating Soil Fauna Guilds to Modulate Weathering Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying an experimental methodology from marine biogeochemistry to the study of soil fauna impacts on weathering. In marine systems, the contributions of different microbial trophic levels to nutrient cycles are disentangled using size-fractionation and selective inhibition techniques. Adapted to soil science, this involves constructing experimental systems where functional guilds of the soil food web (e.g., microbivores, detritivores, predators) are manipulated sequentially—through physical removal, selective biocides, or staged introductions. This guild-level 'fractionation' will quantitatively isolate how each faunal group directly (e.g., via comminution) and indirectly (e.g., by altering microbial community structure and activity) accelerates or modulates silicate mineral weathering processes, addressing the scaling complexity of biotic interactions.

ideas.title: Soil-Informed 3D Microfluidic Mesocosms for Real-Time Visualization of Rhizosphere Processes
ideas.description: This approach constructs microfluidic mesocosms that embed 3D-printed, soil-realistic architectures, moving beyond simple 2D channels. The scaffold geometry is directly informed by micro-CT imaging of natural soil, replicating pore networks and mineral-organic interfaces. A suite of embedded microsensors and optical windows allows for concurrent monitoring of chemical gradients, microbial localization, and mineral surface changes. This creates a more ecologically valid testbed to visualize and quantify how root exudates and microbes navigate and modify a complex physical matrix to drive mineral weathering and nutrient cycling.

ideas.title: Multi-agent microfluidic interrogation of synergistic mineral weathering
ideas.description: This research investigates how the concerted actions of a root hair, a fungal hypha, and a bacterial strain, operating as a minimal consortium, weather a specific mineral surface. Using a transparent microfluidic chip that simulates soil pore networks, we co-culture these organisms with a target mineral grain to observe their physical and chemical interactions in situ. High-resolution chemical mapping and force measurements are taken over time to quantify how the agents' combined chemical exudates and physical forces alter dissolution rates and surface morphology compared to any single agent. This approach reveals the emergent biogeochemical mechanisms that drive weathering in complex natural systems.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Stoichiometry Theory to Weathering Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of studying weathering under controlled, constant nutrient conditions by drawing an analogy from aquatic ecology. Ecological stoichiometry solved the parallel problem of understanding organism growth under fluctuating nutrient ratios by developing theory and methods for dynamic systems. Adapted to weathering research, this involves applying stoichiometric frameworks to design experiments where weathering-derived nutrient availability fluctuates in field-realistic regimes (both in ratio and concentration), tracking how these dynamic chemical imbalances feedback to alter biotic-abiotic interactions and overall weathering rates, moving beyond the artificial stability of laboratory conditions.

ideas.title: Biota-Specific Bioengineering for Diagnostic and Enhanced Weathering
ideas.description: This research direction seeks to bioengineer specific plant-microbe-mineral systems to accelerate silicate weathering for carbon dioxide removal, while deliberately shaping the chemistry of the resultant weathering fluids to carry a diagnostic biosignature. By understanding and manipulating the link between microbial community function and the solute 'fingerprint' they produce, we can design consortia that maximize carbonate precipitation rates and yield a unique chemical signature. This signature acts as a built-in tracer, enabling verification of the engineered carbon sink's activity and persistence, and providing a novel tool for distinguishing biotic from abiotic weathering contributions in terrestrial and potential analogue extraterrestrial systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Microbial Source Tracking to Distinguish Biotic Weathering Fingerprints
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that biotic biosignatures in weathering solutions are always comparable by drawing an analogy from Microbial Source Tracking (MST) in environmental microbiology. MST solves the analogous problem of distinguishing contamination from different biological sources (e.g., human vs. bovine feces) in water by using libraries of host-specific genetic markers and statistical attribution models. The adapted idea proposes to build a similar reference library, but of diagnostic geochemical and isotopic 'fingerprints' measured from weathering solutions produced by distinct biota in controlled laboratory mesocosms. This reference library would then enable the development and application of source attribution models to field-collected weathering solutions, providing a method to test if and how different microbial consortia, plant communities, or soil fauna produce unique, diagnostic chemical signatures that can be disentangled from the abiotic weathering background.

ideas.title: Mapping Soil K Mobility Niches Using a Hydrogeological Contaminant Transport Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniformly poor K mobility in soils by drawing an analogy from hydrogeology, where the transport of solutes (contaminants) is understood to be highly heterogeneous. The analogous solution involves mapping preferential flow paths and sorption domains to predict contaminant movement. Adapted to soil science, the research would conceptualize the soil matrix as a heterogeneous porous medium where K+ mobility is significantly higher in specific niches created by coarse-textured pathways, low-activity mineral zones, or continuous biopores, while being strongly retarded in fine-textured, high-CEC domains. The methodology would adapt hydrogeological tools—tracer tests, geophysical imaging of soil structure, and reactive transport modeling—to identify, quantify, and predict these K mobility niches as a function of measurable soil texture and mineralogy parameters.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Biophysics Techniques to Study Mineral Weathering at the Single-Grain Interface
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from single-molecule biophysics to investigate the fundamental mechanisms of mineral weathering at the single-grain scale. The analogous problem—understanding the precise mechanics and kinetics of individual molecular interactions—is solved using tools like optical tweezers and AFM for force measurement combined with fluorescence or other probes for concurrent chemical observation. The adapted idea applies this dual mechanical-chemical approach to a geobiological context: using a modified AFM or micro-manipulation system to quantify the attachment force and mechanical probing of a single root hair/hypha on a specific mineral surface, while employing nanoscale chemical imaging (e.g., NanoSIMS, confocal Raman microscopy) to visualize and quantify the dissolution trench, ion flux, and precipitate formation at the biotic-abiotic interface in real time. This allows the deconvolution of the physical and biochemical contributions to weathering at the ultimate scale of a single organism-mineral interaction.

ideas.title: Quantify biosignatures for large-scale biogeochemical models
ideas.description: This research aims to parameterize biotic weathering fingerprints for incorporation into Earth system models. The analogy is drawn from astrophysics and exoplanet science, where the solution to detecting biosignatures involves identifying discrete, quantifiable spectral features (like specific gas absorption lines) that serve as fingerprints for atmospheric components, which are then integrated into forward models. Adapting this solution, the research will identify analogous discrete geochemical or isotopic 'fingerprints'—such as specific mineralogical transformation pathways, trace element patterns, or isotopic ratios—that are unambiguously linked to biological activity during weathering. These quantifiable biosignatures will be parameterized and incorporated as tracers or process terms into the geochemical modules of large-scale Earth system models, enabling models to explicitly track and predict the influence of biology on the silicate weathering carbon sink at global scales.

ideas.title: Weathering dynamics under statistically field-validated nutrient and water fluctuation regimes
ideas.description: This study first establishes an empirical basis for 'field-realistic' by conducting multi-year, high-frequency monitoring of soil solution chemistry and moisture at key lithological field sites to define the true statistical properties of natural fluctuations. These properties—including temporal autocorrelation, cross-correlation between water and nutrients, frequency-magnitude distributions, and extreme event thresholds—are then encoded into a control system for laboratory weathering experiments. The experimental mesocosms are subjected to these statistically validated regimes, moving beyond arbitrary sinusoidal or random variations. This approach directly tests how the temporal structure of environmental forcing, not just its mean or range, controls biogeochemical weathering fluxes and microbial community function.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Paleo-Pedotronics: Calibrating Deep-Time Biotic Inferences with Real-Time Rhizosphere Mimics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates microfluidic lab-on-a-chip devices with inverse modeling of paleosol chemistry. It uses the microfluidic platforms to physically simulate the mineral weathering processes driven by specific, hypothesized configurations of ancient roots and microbes. The chemical outputs from these controlled, real-time experiments are then used as a ground-truth dataset to train and test computational models that infer past ecosystem components from weathered regolith signatures. This creates a powerful feedback loop, moving paleopedology from purely correlative inference towards mechanistic, experimentally validated deduction of deep-time biotic activity.

ideas.title: Applying Synchrotron-Based Micro-XANES to Map Solid-Phase Stoichiometry in Regolith Weathering Products
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from soil science to the analysis of solid regolith phases. In environmental geochemistry, synchrotron-based micro-XANES spectroscopy is used to solve the analogous problem of tracking in-situ stoichiometric changes and speciation of elements within complex solid matrices like contaminated soils. The adapted research idea applies this technique to planetary regolith. By using μ-XANES on lunar or Martian simulants subjected to weathering processes, we can directly map changes in the oxidation state and local coordination of critical elements (e.g., Fe, Mn, S) within individual solid phases like nanophase iron or incipient clays. This provides a direct measure of elemental retention and transformation in the weathering products themselves, offering a spatially resolved understanding of stoichiometry changes that leachate analysis alone cannot reveal.

ideas.title: Spatially Resolved Functional Profiling of Biotic Weathering Agents via In Situ Spectroscopic Imaging
ideas.description: This research direction integrates systematic biotic component isolation with advanced spectroscopic imaging (e.g., Raman, FTIR) to achieve spatially resolved, in situ functional profiling of weathering agents. By applying spectroscopic mapping to controlled experiments with individual or consortia of microbes, fungi, and roots, it becomes possible to visualize and quantify the specific chemical modifications (e.g., organic acid secretion, mineral dissolution, secondary mineral formation) each agent produces at the microbe-mineral interface. This approach transforms the study of biotic weathering from a bulk, correlative analysis into a direct, mechanistic investigation of agent-specific biogeochemical function, revealing how different biological actors partition their roles and potentially synergize within a weathering microenvironment.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Upscaling Framework for Biotic Weathering in Watershed Models
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a proven scaling framework from ecology to solve the challenge of integrating rock-specific biotic weathering into landscape models. In ecology, leaf-level physiology is scaled to canopy and ecosystem levels using trait distributions and environmental gradients. Analogously, we propose to develop a hierarchical upscaling framework where microbial weathering 'traits' and process rates, measured at the rock interface, are aggregated based on the spatial heterogeneity of the critical zone (lithology, fracture networks, soil depth) and hydrologic flow paths. This adapted approach will create a transferable sub-model to dynamically represent biotic weathering contributions within catchment-scale hydro-biogeochemical models, moving beyond simplistic, homogeneous rate constants.

ideas.title: In Situ Spectroscopic Tracking of Biotic Deceleration in Mineral Carbonation
ideas.description: This research direction employs in situ spectroscopic imaging (Raman/FTIR mapping) to characterize how specific biotic interactions and successional stages within mineral-associated biofilms actively decelerate CO2 mineralization rates. By spatially correlating chemical maps of carbonate precipitation with spectroscopic signatures of microbial community composition, metabolic activity, and extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) chemistry, the approach can identify the precise biotic mechanisms—such as EPS buffering, preferential acid production, or competitive mineral dissolution—that inhibit carbonation. This enables a direct, mechanistic test of the hypothesis that ecosystem complexity does not always accelerate, and can sometimes strategically retard, carbon sequestration in mineral systems.

ideas.title: Decadal-scale lithological chronosequences for biosignature stability and false-positive prediction
ideas.description: This research establishes a multi-decadal experimental chronosequence using a diverse suite of rocks, including carbonates, evaporites, ultramafics, and iron formations, beyond common igneous and metamorphic types. By subjecting these lithologies to controlled, long-term weathering simulations, the study will track how complex mineralogical assemblages differentially alter, trap, degrade, or produce abiotic organic signatures over time. This approach uniquely reveals lithology-specific 'windows of biosignature detectability' and false-positive formation rates that are invisible in short-term studies or limited rock sets. The resulting dataset will provide a time-resolved, lithology-dependent matrix essential for interpreting planetary surface records and prioritizing targets for life detection missions.

ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from pharmacology for studying drug interactions to systematically decouple individual biotic component effects on weathering. The analogous problem—isolating the effect of a single drug within a combination therapy—is solved using factorial experimental designs and interaction analysis. By implementing a full factorial mesocosm experiment where microbes, fungi, and plants are independently manipulated as presence/absence treatments, and by applying statistical models to parse main effects and interaction terms from weathering rate data, the specific, quantifiable role of each biological agent, as well as their interdependencies, can be rigorously isolated for the first time.

ideas.title: Establish a Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) Network for Planetary Weathering and Biosignature Evolution
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of studying long-term (multi-year to decadal) weathering dynamics and biosignature evolution by adapting the solution from terrestrial ecology: the Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) Network. In ecology, the LTER framework overcame the short-term nature of typical grants by creating a network of sites with standardized observation protocols, sustained funding, and centralized data management for decadal-scale studies. Adapted to planetary science, this would involve establishing a 'Planetary Weathering and Biosignature LTER Network.' This network would deploy standardized, instrumented experimental arrays containing relevant rock and regolith simulants, synthetic organics, and potential biosignature carriers at multiple key analog sites on Earth (e.g., the Atacama Desert, Arctic permafrost, hydrothermal fields). The arrays would be designed for long-term durability with minimal maintenance, automated environmental monitoring, and secure data telemetry. A central governing body would coordinate periodic, detailed sampling and laboratory analysis campaigns across all sites on a fixed schedule (e.g., every 2-5 years), using consistent analytical techniques to build comparable, multi-decadal time-series datasets. This approach moves beyond isolated, short-duration experiments to create a sustained, systematic observatory for the processes critical to interpreting planetary surface records and the long-term fate of biosignatures.

ideas.title: Paleoecosystem Tomography: Reconstructing Past Biotic Networks from Geochemical Traces
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem methodology from cosmology, where the cosmic microwave background is used to deduce the early universe's composition and physical laws, to the field of paleoecology. The analogous solution involves using forward models of system evolution (cosmic or pedogenic) coupled with Bayesian statistical inversion to constrain historical parameters from a remnant chemical signature. The adapted idea develops a geochemical-biological forward model that simulates how different hypothetical paleo-ecosystems (defined by their functional biotic components) would alter regolith weathering profiles over time. By applying Bayesian inversion (e.g., MCMC sampling) to real paleosol chemistry data, the model statistically infers the most probable historical ecosystem structure and key biotic processes, effectively performing a 'tomographic reconstruction' of past life from its geochemical shadow.

ideas.title: Systematic lithological sampling using a phylogenetic framework from ecology
ideas.description: The original idea to expand the rock set beyond granite, rhyolite, schist, and basalt is analogous to the problem in ecology of sampling biodiversity beyond a few common or model organisms. Ecologists solved this by using a phylogenetic framework to guide sampling across the tree of life, ensuring representation of major evolutionary lineages and their distinct traits. Adapting this solution, we propose constructing a conceptual 'lithological phylogeny' based on genetic/mineralogical lineages (e.g., primary mantle differentiation, crustal melting series, sedimentary provenance and diagenesis, metamorphic facies series). The research would then systematically select representative end-member and transitional rock samples from the major branches and nodes of this framework for experimental testing. This principled, process-based expansion beyond an ad-hoc list will robustly constrain the broader lithological controls by ensuring the sample set captures the fundamental diversity-generating processes in rock formation.

ideas.title: Context-Dependent Probabilistic Mapping of Biotic Weathering Signatures for Conditional ESMs
ideas.description: This research establishes a framework to quantify how biotic weathering fingerprints are contingent on local lithological and environmental context, preventing the misapplication of universal parameters. It combines controlled mesocosm experiments across a matrix of rock types and climates with inverse geochemical modeling to produce probabilistic relationships between biological processes and weathering signals. These relationships are structured as conditional parameterizations, where the applicable biotic weathering function and its uncertainty are determined by real-time model classifiers of substrate and climate. The output is a modular, context-aware weathering module that dynamically selects appropriate biotic algorithms, significantly improving model fidelity across diverse terrestrial environments.

ideas.title: Employ Correlative Spectroscopic Imaging for Mineral-Biofilm Interface Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the correlative imaging workflow from biomedical histopathology to the study of mineral-biofilm interfaces. In histopathology, FTIR or Raman spectral maps are overlaid with high-resolution optical images to link chemistry to morphology. The adapted solution involves applying Raman or FTIR mapping to a mineral-biofilm sample to characterize the chemical composition in situ, then precisely registering this spectroscopic data with a structural image from a complementary technique like SEM or confocal laser scanning microscopy. This correlative approach will directly link the chemical signatures of mineral dissolution, secondary mineral formation, and biofilm metabolic products to the physical microstructure and organization of the biofilm at the interface, providing a powerful multi-modal characterization method.

ideas.title: Parametric rock suites to isolate functional controls of target geochemical or physical properties
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond simply expanding a rock set to strategically constructing experimental suites that test specific mechanistic hypotheses. For a defined target property, rocks are selected to independently vary key mineralogical or textural parameters across a realistic range, such as carbonate content, clay type and abundance, or porosity. This parametric design allows for statistical modeling of the property as a function of these compositional variables, constraining their individual and interactive effects. The result is a predictive framework that identifies dominant lithological controls and their underlying mechanisms, rather than a mere catalog of observations.

ideas.title: Multi-Generational Legacy Framework for Securing Decadal-Scale Weathering and Biosignature Data
ideas.description: This redesigned approach establishes a persistent, low-maintenance observation network on a planetary surface, rather than a single long-duration experiment. It deploys standardized, passive experimental modules containing sample coupons and environmental sensors designed for decadal-scale exposure. The key innovation is a planned handover protocol, where data and physical samples are periodically retrieved, analyzed, and archived by subsequent missions, creating a continuous, secure timeline. This framework ensures robust data collection across mission generations, directly overcoming the risk of total loss from a single point of failure.
